```
  |  SIDE SETBACK 1.5m                       SIDE SETBACK 1.5m   |
  |  +----------------------------------------------------+      |
  |  | RECEPTION (5x4)  |  WAITING (5x4)  | PANTRY (3x3)  |     |
  |  +------------------+-----------------+---------------+     |
  |  |  MGR CABIN (4x4.5) | CONF ROOM (6x6) | STORE (3x3) |     |
  |  +--------------------+-----------------+-------------+     |
  |  | WORK BAY-1 (4.5x6) | WORK BAY-2 (4.5x6) | TOILET  |     |
  |  +--------------------+----------------------+--------+     |
  |  | STAIR/FIRE ESCAPE  |  CORRIDOR 2.0m WIDE  | LIFT  |     |
  |  +--------------------+----------------------+-------+     |
  |  +----------------------------------------------------+    |
  |      FRONT SETBACK 3.0m (with road of 9m)                 |
  +--------------------------------------------------------------+
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is **schematic** (not to exact scale). For ESE submission, students must dimension every wall, room, and overall boundary, and indicate **plinth lines, setback lines, and road kerb** clearly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**List any six mandatory components that must be shown on the PLAN drawing of a public building.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The plan of a public building must mandatorily show:
1. **Outer wall lines and partition walls** (with thickness indicated)
2. **Door and window positions** with proper opening arcs and frame lines
3. **Room dimensions** (inner clear dimensions) and **overall outer dimensions**
4. **Furniture layout** (tables, chairs, counters) to convey function
5. **Staircase** with direction of flight (up/down arrows) and flight dimensions
6. **North arrow, scale (graphical), title block, and section line (A–A)** along with **setback lines** and **road/plot boundary**

> [!NOTE]
> *Each correct point carries 0.5 marks; complete enumeration needed for full 3 marks.*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Differentiate between PLAN, SECTION, and ELEVATION of a building with one key drawing convention for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Drawing | Cut/View Type | One Key Convention |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Plan** | Horizontal cut at **1.2 m** above plinth | Cut walls shown with **45° cross-hatching**; doors shown as quarter-circle arc |
| **Section** | Vertical cut through the building | Shows **floor levels, roof line, foundation depth**; cut walls hatched |
| **Elevation** | External vertical view, no cut | **No hatching**; shows facade finishes, sunshades, parapet |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]
**(a)** Plan a **Bank Building (G+1)** on a **30 m × 20 m** plot with a 9 m wide road on the south side. Draw the **PLAN** to a scale of **1:100** and justify the location of each functional unit. **7 marks**

**(b)** Draw the **SECTION (A–A)** and **FRONT ELEVATION** of the same bank to a scale of **1:100**. Mark all levels, dimensions, and finishes. **7 marks**

### **Model Solution for Q A (a) — PLAN [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Statutory checks (1 mark)**
- Plot = 30 × 20 = 600 m²
- FSI assumed = 1.5, so total permissible floor area = 900 m²
- Max coverage = 40 %, so footprint ≤ 240 m²
- Setbacks: Front 3 m, Rear 2 m, Sides 1.5 m each
- Buildable envelope = 27 m × 17 m → adopt **18 m × 14 m** footprint (252 m² → adjust to 17 m × 14 m = 238 m² for 39.7 % coverage)

**Step 2: Functional grouping (1.5 marks)**
- **Public Zone:** Reception, Lobby, Public Toilet
- **Semi-public:** Cash counters, Locker enquiry
- **Private:** Manager cabin, Staff bay, Strong room
- **Service:** Stair, Lift, Pantry, Store, Server

**Step 3: Bubble to Block conversion (1.5 marks)**

| Room | Size (m) | Location Logic |
|------|----------|----------------|
| Reception + Lobby | 5 × 6 | Front-centre, near entry |
| Cash Counter | 4 × 5 | Behind reception, secure |
| Manager Cabin | 4 × 4.5 | Adjacent to cash, rear side |
| Strong Room | 3 × 3 | Rear corner, 1.2 m RC walls |
| Locker Room | 4 × 5 | Near cash, semi-secure |
| Records | 4 × 4 | Adjacent to manager |
| Staff Toilet | 1.5 × 2.1 | Service core |
| Public Toilet | 1.5 × 2.1 | Near lobby, accessible |
| Stair + Lift | 4 × 6 | Side core |

**Step 4: Plan drawing (2 marks)**
- Walls: 230 mm outer / 115 mm partition
- Doors: 1.0 m / 1.2 m wide, 90° swing
- Windows: 1.2 m × 1.5 m on external walls
- North arrow top-right, scale 1:100, title block bottom-right
- Section line A–A passing through entry, cash, stair
- Dimension chains: inner clear + outer overall

**Step 5: Justification (1 mark)**
- Lobby at front for direct public access
- Cash and Locker behind lobby for security
- Manager cabin close to cash for supervision
- Strong room in rear corner (max. structural isolation)
- Stair/lift core on side for vertical circulation without disturbing transactions

### **Model Solution for Q A (b) — SECTION + ELEVATION [7 marks]**

**SECTION A–A (3.5 marks)**

| Element | Level (m) | Notes |
|---------|-----------|-------|
| Ground level | 0.00 | Reference datum |
| Plinth | +0.45 | Min. KMBR, 600 mm preferred for banks |
| Sill | +0.90 | Window start |
| Lintel | +2.10 | Window/door top |
| Slab bottom (FFL+slab) | +3.30 | Floor-to-floor 3.0 m + 0.30 m slab |
| Roof finish | +3.45 | Weathering course 150 mm |
| Parapet top | +4.05 | 600 mm parapet |
| Foundation bottom | −1.20 | For laterite/sandy soil Kerala |

Show: plinth beam, lintel band, roof slab, weathering course, stair flight (riser 150 mm, tread 250 mm), door/window heights, foundation in PCC 1:3:6 below brickwork.

**FRONT ELEVATION (3.5 marks)**

- Project from plan (lines dropped vertically from corners)
- Show: parapet 600 mm, sunshades 600 mm at lintel, bands at sill/lintel
- Windows: 1.2 m × 1.5 m with glazed shutters
- Main door: 1.8 m × 2.1 m double-leaf
- Finish indication: cream/yellow paint, stone cladding at base
- Floor levels (GL, PL, FFL) marked with dotted lines and labels
- Overall height dimension chain on the right

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]
**(a)** Plan a **Public Health Centre (PHC)** for a rural area on a **25 m × 18 m** plot. The PHC should have a registration counter, two consulting rooms, a waiting hall, a pharmacy, a small lab, an injection/dressing room, and toilets. Draw the **PLAN at 1:100**. **7 marks**

**(b)** Draw the **SECTION** and **SIDE ELEVATION** of the same PHC at 1:100. **7 marks**

### **Model Solution Outline for Q B (a) — PHC PLAN [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Layout strategy (2 marks)**
- Adopt a **single-loaded corridor** (2.0 m wide) with rooms on one side and waiting/external verandah on the other for natural cross-ventilation
- **Entry from the south** (front road), ambulance bay on the east side
- Apply setbacks: 3 m front, 1.5 m sides, 2 m rear

**Step 2: Room sizes and placement (2 marks)**

| Room | Size (m) | Location |
|------|----------|----------|
| Registration | 3.6 × 3.6 | Adjacent to entry |
| Waiting Hall | 5.0 × 6.0 | Central, visible from entry |
| Consulting 1 | 3.6 × 4.2 | Quiet side, near waiting |
| Consulting 2 | 3.6 × 4.2 | Adjacent to consulting 1 |
| Injection/Dressing | 3.0 × 3.6 | Near consulting rooms |
| Lab (collection) | 3.0 × 3.6 | Far end, less public access |
| Pharmacy | 3.6 × 4.0 | Near exit, accessible to public |
| Toilets (M/F/Staff) | 1.5 × 2.1 each | Rear corner, isolated |

**Step 3: Plan drawing with doors, windows, dimensions (3 marks)**
- 230 mm outer walls, 115 mm partitions
- Main entry 1.5 m double-leaf; room doors 1.0 m single
- Windows 1.2 × 1.5 m for cross-ventilation (1/6 of floor area)
- Stair/lift if G+1; otherwise indicate future provision
- North arrow, scale, title block, section line

### **Model Solution Outline for Q B (b) — PHC SECTION + ELEVATION [7 marks]**

**SECTION (3.5 marks):** Show plinth 0.45 m, sill 0.9 m, lintel 2.1 m, roof 3.45 m, parapet 0.6 m, foundation depth 1.2 m. Hatch walls, label floor levels, indicate ramp at entry (1:12 slope for ambulances/wheelchairs).

**SIDE ELEVATION (3.5 marks):** Show waiting hall verandah, sunshades, bands, ramp with handrail, main entry porch, finish schedule. Dimension overall height.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the North Arrow** — 0.5 mark deduction (very common!)
> 2. **Not showing setbacks on the plan** — KMBR compliance is mandatory
> 3. **Section line not lettered (A–A) or arrows missing** — full 1 mark can be lost
> 4. **Elevation not projected from the plan** — drawing must align vertically with plan
> 5. **No dimensioning of room sizes** — examiners expect inner clear dimensions
> 6. **Confusing section and elevation** — remember: section is **CUT** (with hatching), elevation is **VIEW** (no hatching)
> 7. **Missing scale (graphical)** — both numerical (1:100) and graphical bar scale required
> 8. **Title block incomplete** — must have student name, register number, date, sheet title

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam**

- **Definition:** Public buildings serve the community — institutional, administrative, commercial, or healthcare functions.
- **Building Types to choose ANY TWO:** Office complex, PHC, Commercial, Educational, Post Office, Bank.
- **Three mandatory drawings:** PLAN (1.2 m above plinth cut), SECTION (vertical cut A–A), ELEVATION (external view, no hatching).
- **KMBR 2019 Key Setbacks:** Front 3 m, Rear 2 m, Sides 1.5 m (default for plots < 1000 m²).
- **Head room (public bldg.):** **3.0 m** standard; min. 2.75 m.
- **Plinth height:** **450 mm** min. above road crown/ground.
- **Door widths:** Main entry **1.5–1.8 m**; room doors **1.0 m**; toilet doors **0.8 m**.
- **Stair:** Tread **250 mm**, Riser **150 mm** (2T + R = 600 mm rule).
- **Corridor width:** Min. **2.0 m** for public buildings.
- **Window area:** **1/6** of floor area for adequate daylight and ventilation.
- **Tools workflow:** Bubble diagram → Adjacency matrix → Block diagram → Walls/Doors/Windows → Dimensions → Furniture → Hatching → Title block.
- **FSI for semi-urban Kerala:** typically **1.0 to 1.5**; coverage **35–40 %**.
- **Strong room (Bank):** RC walls 1.2 m thick, separate ventilation, fire-resistant door.
- **PHC:** Must have ramp access (1:12 slope), separate bio-waste route, hand-wash basin in every consulting room.
- **Office:** Modular grid of **4.5 m × 6.0 m** is industry standard.
- **Section conventions:** Cut walls hatched 45°, ground line as a sloped wedge (30°), roof with weathering course.
- **Elevation conventions:** No hatching, projection lines from plan must be perfectly vertical, finish legend included.
- **Title block must include:** Title, Scale, North arrow, Date, Student name, Reg. No.
- **Examiner's pet peeves:** Missing north arrow, no setbacks shown, hatching missing in section, dimensions crossed over, plan not at 1.2 m level.
- **Always verify:** Coverage %, FSI, setback compliance, fire escape (if height > 15 m), parking ECS.
- **Final tip:** Practice drawing **at least 2 full sets (Plan + Section + Elevation)** for a Bank and a PHC before the exam — these are the most frequently set KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plan and prepare plumbing and sanitary drawings of a building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL507 - CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Plan and prepare plumbing and sanitary drawings of a building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plumbing Drawing**: A technical drawing that graphically represents the complete network of pipes, fittings, fixtures, valves, and appurtenances required to convey water supply into a building and to convey the soil and waste water out of the building, prepared in accordance with **IS 1742:1983** (Code of Practice for Building Drainage) and **IS 2065:1983** (Code of Practice for Water Supply in Buildings).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanitary Drawing**: A specialised subset of plumbing drawings that focuses exclusively on the disposal of sewage and sullage, including the layout of soil pipes, waste pipes, vent pipes, manholes, inspection chambers, septic tanks, and the final connection to the municipal sewer or soak pit, governed by **IS 5329:1983** and the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016), Part 9**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the laboratory exercise on "planning and preparing plumbing and sanitary drawings" is a **Computer-Aided Design (CAD) / Manual Drafting** task in which the student is expected to:
1. Read a given architectural plan (typically a residential 1BHK/2BHK or small institutional building).
2. Fix the positions of all sanitary fixtures (WC, washbasin, sink, bath tub, urinal, etc.).
3. Route the supply pipes (rising main, distribution lines) and the discharge pipes (soil, waste, vent, rain water).
4. Indicate pipe diameters, gradients, traps, manholes, and septic tank.
5. Prepare the **plan view**, **sectional elevation**, and the **schematic isometric** of the entire system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Building as a Living Body**
> Imagine a building is a **human body**. The **plumbing system** is the **circulatory system**, the **sanitary system** is the **excretory system**, and the **vent pipes** are the **lungs (breathing)**.
> - **Fresh water enters** through arteries (rising main/distribution pipes).
> - **Used water exits** through veins (drainage pipes) to a "kidney" (septic tank / sewer).
> - The **traps** are the **valves of the heart** — they prevent bad gases (sewer gases) from returning into the building.
> - Just as a cardiologist draws a **circulation diagram** before surgery, a civil engineer draws a **plumbing/sanitary drawing** before construction to ensure every drop of water has a clear, hygienic path.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions

- **Drawing Scale (Plan)**: 1 : 50 or 1 : 100 (as per **IS 962:1989**).
- **Drawing Scale (Detail)**: 1 : 20 or 1 : 10.
- **Standard pipe diameters (mm) for residential buildings**:
  - **15 mm (½")** – branch to individual tap
  - **20 mm (¾")** – branch to flush tank
  - **25 mm (1")** – main distribution
  - **40 mm (1½")** – waste pipe from washbasin/bath
  - **50 mm (2")** – soil/waste stack
  - **75 mm (3")** – Urinal / branch soil pipe
  - **100 mm (4")** – Main soil stack / building drain
  - **150 mm (6")** – External sewer / manhole
- **Minimum gradient for horizontal drains**:
  - **100 mm dia** → 1 in 60
  - **150 mm dia** → 1 in 100
  - **225 mm dia and above** → 1 in 120 to 1 in 200
- **Trap seal depth**: minimum **50 mm** (to prevent siphonage of the water seal).
- **Vent pipe extension above terrace**: minimum **900 mm** (or 600 mm above the highest window if within 3 m).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Plumbing Systems

The KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests the student's ability to **identify and draw** the four classical systems of building drainage. The choice of system determines the entire pipe routing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Standard Systems of Plumbing (as per IS 5329:1983)**

| System | Description | Application | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Stack System** | One vertical pipe carries both soil AND waste. No separate vent pipe. | Low-rise residential, economy housing. | Economical, fewer pipes. | High risk of siphonage / cross-flow. |
| **One-Pipe System** | Single stack for soil + waste, with a **partial vent** (vent cowl at top). | 2 to 3 storey buildings. | Better than single stack. | Ventilation is partial. |
| **One-Pipe Partially Ventilated System** | Single stack + a separate **vent pipe** (anti-siphon pipe) for the upper floors. | Multi-storey buildings (4–6 floors). | Reliable for moderate height. | More pipes than one-pipe. |
| **Two-Pipe System** | Separate **soil pipe** (for WC) and **waste pipe** (for bath, basin, sink). Each has its own vent stack. | Hospitals, hotels, high-rise buildings. | Most hygienic and reliable. | Costly; more pipe routing. |

### 2.2 Components of a Sanitary Drawing

Every sanitary drawing in the KTU lab must clearly show:

1. **Sanitary Fixtures** – WC (Indian/European), Wash Basin, Kitchen Sink, Bath Tub, Shower, Urinal, Bidet.
2. **Traps** – Floor Trap (FT), Gully Trap (GT), P-Trap, S-Trap, Intercepting Trap, Bottle Trap.
3. **Pipes** – Soil Pipe (SP), Waste Pipe (WP), Vent Pipe (VP), Anti-siphon Pipe (ASP), Rain Water Pipe (RWP), Supply Pipe.
4. **Fittings** – Bend, Tee, Cross, Elbow, Reducer, Junction, Cleanout Plug.
5. **External Works** – Manhole (MH), Inspection Chamber (IC), Septic Tank (ST), Soak Pit, Sewer Line, Vent Shaft.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are testable in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions of the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination.

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \times V$ | Discharge through a pipe (continuity equation) | $Q$ in L/s, $A$ in m², $V$ in m/s |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ | **Manning's Equation** for gravity flow in drains | $V$ in m/s, $R$ in m, $S$ dimensionless, $n$ = 0.013 for PVC / 0.015 for stoneware |
| 3 | $V = 0.8492 \cdot C \cdot R^{0.63} \cdot S^{0.54}$ | **Hazen-Williams Equation** for water supply in pipes | $C$ = 130 (PVC), 100 (CI), $R$ in m, $S$ in m/m |
| 4 | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi V}}$ | Pipe diameter from discharge | $D$ in m |
| 5 | $R_h = \dfrac{A}{P}$ | Hydraulic radius (for partially filled circular pipe) | $A$ = wetted area, $P$ = wetted perimeter |
| 6 | $Q_{des} = P \times q_{pc}$ | Design discharge for water supply | $P$ = population, $q_{pc}$ = per capita demand (**135 LPCD** for residential) |
| 7 | $V_{flush} = 0.0104 \cdot L^{0.5}$ (or simply $V \geq 0.6$ m/s) | **Self-cleansing velocity** in sewer | $L$ = flow depth in mm, minimum **0.6 m/s**, max **3.0 m/s** |
| 8 | $S_{min} = \dfrac{1}{N} = \dfrac{1}{60}$ for 100 mm dia | Minimum gradient of drain (1 in $N$) | $N$ depends on diameter |
| 9 | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | **Darcy-Weisbach** head loss | $f$ = friction factor, $L, D$ in m |
| 10 | $W_{FU} = \sum (n_i \times w_i)$ | Total **Fixture Units** (Hunter's method) | $n_i$ = no. of fixtures, $w_i$ = unit weight |
| 11 | $\text{Trap seal depth} \geq 50 \text{ mm}$ | Anti-siphonage rule | mm |
| 12 | $\text{Vent extension above roof} \geq 900 \text{ mm}$ | NBC 2016 rule | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Rule for KTU Exam**: The self-cleansing velocity of **0.6 m/s** is a **highly repeated KTU question**. A velocity below 0.6 m/s causes **silting** (solid deposition). Above 3.0 m/s causes **scouring** (pipe erosion). This range is **inviolable** for KTU 2024.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Construction Industry**: Every building plan submitted for KTU/municipal approval must include plumbing and sanitary drawings.
- **Public Health**: Incorrect trap design or missing vents lead to **sewer gas infiltration** → diseases (typhoid, cholera, hepatitis).
- **Water Conservation**: Proper pipe sizing reduces **water hammer**, leakage, and energy consumption in pumping.
- **BIM (Building Information Modeling)**: Modern AutoCAD-REVIT plumbing modules (MEP) directly use these conventions to auto-generate **BOM (Bill of Materials)** and **clash detection** with structural drawings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Manning's Equation Applied to a Building Drain

The KTU 2024 examiner frequently tests the application of Manning's equation to a partially filled circular sewer. The full derivation (commonly asked for 7 marks) is given below.

**Step 1: Statement of Manning's Empirical Equation**

$$V = \frac{1}{n} \cdot R_h^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

where:
- $V$ = velocity of flow in m/s
- $n$ = Manning's roughness coefficient
- $R_h$ = hydraulic radius = $A / P$ in metres
- $S$ = slope of the energy grade line ≈ bed slope for uniform flow

> *Logic*: Manning's equation was empirically fitted by the Irish engineer **Robert Manning (1890)** from over 1000 observations. The exponent $2/3$ on $R$ and $1/2$ on $S$ emerged from regression on field data.

**Step 2: Substitute the Continuity Equation $Q = A \cdot V$**

Multiplying both sides by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$$Q = A \cdot \frac{1}{n} \cdot R_h^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

This is the most commonly used form for sizing building drains.

**Step 3: For a Circular Pipe Flowing Full**

For a pipe of diameter $D$:
- Area $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- Wetted perimeter $P = \pi D$
- $R_h = \dfrac{A}{P} = \dfrac{\pi D^2 / 4}{\pi D} = \dfrac{D}{4}$

Substitute $R_h = D/4$:

$$Q_{full} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{n} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{4}\right)^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the Expression**

$$Q_{full} = \frac{\pi}{4 \cdot 4^{2/3} \cdot n} \cdot D^{2 + 2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

$$Q_{full} = \frac{\pi}{4^{5/3} \cdot n} \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

Numerical constant: $4^{5/3} \approx 10.079$

$$Q_{full} \approx \frac{0.3117}{n} \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

> *Logic*: This is the **"full-flow discharge"** formula. For a building drain that is typically designed to flow at **half-full or 2/3 full** (to allow for air circulation and prevent surcharge), we multiply by a partial-flow correction factor $q^*$ from standard hydraulic charts.

**Step 5: Apply to a Worked KTU Problem**

> **Worked Example**: A 150 mm diameter stoneware drain ($n = 0.015$) is laid at a gradient of 1 in 100. Determine the discharge and velocity when the drain flows full.

**Given**: $D = 0.150$ m, $n = 0.015$, $S = 1/100 = 0.01$

**Step 5a: Area**
$$A = \frac{\pi (0.150)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0225}{4} = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5b: Hydraulic radius (full pipe)**
$$R_h = \frac{D}{4} = \frac{0.150}{4} = 0.0375 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5c: Velocity (Manning's)**
$$V = \frac{1}{0.015} \times (0.0375)^{2/3} \times (0.01)^{1/2}$$

Compute $(0.0375)^{2/3}$:
- $\ln(0.0375) = -3.283$
- $\frac{2}{3} \times (-3.283) = -2.189$
- $e^{-2.189} = 0.1119$

Compute $(0.01)^{1/2} = 0.1$

$$V = 66.667 \times 0.1119 \times 0.1 = 0.7458 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5d: Discharge**
$$Q = A \times V = 0.01767 \times 0.7458 = 0.01318 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 13.18 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 5e: Sanity Check**
$V = 0.7458$ m/s is between **0.6 m/s** (self-cleansing minimum) and **3.0 m/s** (scouring maximum) → **design is safe**. ✅

### 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum Drain Gradient (1 in 60 for 100 mm)

KTU's 2024 syllabus often asks: "Why is the minimum gradient for a 100 mm soil pipe fixed at 1 in 60?"

**Step 1: Self-Cleansing Condition**
A drain is self-cleansing when the tractive force on the solid particles exceeds the critical shear stress to move them. For a 100 mm pipe flowing half-full, the empirical self-cleansing formula is:

$$S_{min} = \frac{1}{D \text{ (in mm)}} \times \text{constant}$$

For organic solids in a 100 mm drain, the empirically adopted constant (from **IS 1742:1983, Clause 4.4**) is:

$$S_{min} = \frac{1}{60}$$

**Step 2: Verify with Manning's Equation at Half-Full**

For $d/D = 0.5$ (half-full), from partial-flow charts:
- $q/Q_{full} = 0.5$ (i.e., $q^* = 0.5$)
- $v/V_{full} = 1.0$ (i.e., $v^* = 1.0$)
- Hydraulic radius at half-full $R_h = D/4$ (same as full)

**Step 3: Set $V_{half-full} = 0.6$ m/s and Solve for $S$**

$$0.6 = \frac{1}{n} \cdot R_h^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

For $n = 0.015$, $D = 0.100$ m, $R_h = 0.025$ m:

$$0.6 = \frac{1}{0.015} \times (0.025)^{2/3} \times S^{1/2}$$

$(0.025)^{2/3} = 0.0855$

$$0.6 = 66.667 \times 0.0855 \times S^{1/2}$$

$$0.6 = 5.700 \times S^{1/2}$$

$$S^{1/2} = 0.1053 \Rightarrow S = 0.01108 \approx \frac{1}{90}$$

Since the design codes use a **factor of safety of 1.5**, the code-mandated minimum is $\dfrac{1}{60}$ — which yields a more conservative (steeper) slope. This is exactly why **IS 1742 fixes 1 in 60 for 100 mm**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Plumbing Systems (for KTU 14-Mark Answers)

| Parameter | Single Stack | One-Pipe | One-Pipe Partially Ventilated | Two-Pipe |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| No. of vertical stacks | 1 | 1 | 1 + 1 vent | 2 + 2 vents |
| Soil & waste carried in | Same pipe | Same pipe | Same pipe | Different pipes |
| Vent pipe | None | Cowl only | Anti-siphon pipe | Full vent stack |
| Risk of siphonage | **High** | Moderate | **Low** | **Very Low** |
| Cost (relative) | **Lowest** | Low | Moderate | **Highest** |
| Recommended for | Economy housing | 2–3 storey | 4–6 storey | Hospitals / High-rise |
| KTU exam weightage | High | High | High | High |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are **simulated Past Year KTU University Exam questions** modelled on the 2024 Scheme pattern. Marks are distributed as per the official KTU ESE pattern: **Part A (3 marks each)** and **Part B (14 marks each with internal choice)**.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a single stack system and a one-pipe partially ventilated system of plumbing. Which one is preferred for a 5-storey residential building and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Single Stack System | One-Pipe Partially Ventilated System |
|---|---|---|
| Soil and waste discharge | Combined in a single stack | Combined in a single stack |
| Vent pipe | **Not provided** | **Anti-siphon pipe provided** along the stack |
| Siphonage risk | **High** (trap seals broken) | **Low** (air admitted via vent) |
| Cost | **Lowest** | **Moderate** |
| Suitable for | Bungalows, 1–2 storey | **Multi-storey (4–6 storey)** |

**Preferred system for 5-storey building**: **One-pipe partially ventilated system** — because the 5-storey height creates significant **negative pressure** in the stack during peak discharge, which can siphon the water seal of upper-floor traps. The anti-siphon vent pipe admits air, breaking the vacuum and protecting the traps.

---

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the function of a gully trap and a floor trap. Why is the gully trap provided at the building face, just before the inspection chamber?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Gully Trap (GT)**: A small masonry or cast-iron chamber fitted at the **outside face of the building wall**, just before the inspection chamber. It collects the discharge from kitchen sinks, bath wastes, and rain water pipes. It contains a **water seal of 50–75 mm** that prevents the foul sewer gas from entering the building.
- **Floor Trap (FT)**: A small cast-iron or PVC trap installed in the **floor of the bathroom / kitchen**, with a stainless steel grating. It collects waste water from the floor and connects to the waste stack. It also has a water seal of **50 mm**.
- **Why at the building face?**: The gully trap is deliberately placed **outside the building line** so that the inspection chamber and the deeper municipal sewer remain isolated from the living spaces. If the municipal sewer surcharges, the gully trap's water seal acts as a **second line of defence**, preventing foul gas from entering the building. It also allows the building's external drainage to be **disconnected** for maintenance without affecting internal plumbing.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule**: For every 14-mark question, the paper setter provides an **internal choice** (either-or). Students are expected to answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each question is split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### Question A. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** A 100 mm diameter stoneware building drain is laid at a gradient of 1 in 80. Assuming the drain flows **full**, determine:
  (i) the velocity of flow using Manning's equation, and
  (ii) the discharge in litres per second.
  Take Manning's $n = 0.015$.
  Verify whether the self-cleansing condition is satisfied. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **two-pipe system of plumbing**. List **four advantages** and **two disadvantages** of this system. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (Question A):**

##### Part (a) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- Pipe diameter $D = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m
- Gradient $S = 1/80 = 0.0125$
- Manning's $n = 0.015$
- Flow condition: Full

**Step 1: Cross-sectional Area (full pipe)** [1 mark]

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.100)^2}{4} = \frac{0.031416}{4} = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Hydraulic Radius (full pipe)** [1 mark]

$$R_h = \frac{D}{4} = \frac{0.100}{4} = 0.025 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Apply Manning's Equation for Velocity** [2 marks]

$$V = \frac{1}{n} \cdot R_h^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

$$V = \frac{1}{0.015} \times (0.025)^{2/3} \times (0.0125)^{1/2}$$

Compute each term:
- $(0.025)^{2/3} = 0.0855$
- $(0.0125)^{1/2} = 0.1118$

$$V = 66.667 \times 0.0855 \times 0.1118 = 0.6373 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Discharge in L/s** [2 marks]

$$Q = A \times V = 0.007854 \times 0.6373 = 0.005005 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q = 0.005005 \times 1000 = 5.005 \text{ L/s} \approx 5.0 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 5: Self-Cleansing Verification** [1 mark]

Minimum self-cleansing velocity required = **0.6 m/s** (as per IS 1742).
Calculated velocity $V = 0.6373$ m/s $> 0.6$ m/s.
Maximum scouring velocity = **3.0 m/s**.
$0.6373$ m/s $< 3.0$ m/s.

**Conclusion**: The drain is **self-cleansing** and **non-scouring**. The design is **safe** and **acceptable**. ✅

---

##### Part (b) — Two-Pipe System Sketch and Description [7 marks]

**Description (4 marks):**
The **two-pipe system of plumbing** is a sanitary arrangement in which the **soil waste** (from water closets) and the **sullage / liquid waste** (from washbasins, baths, sinks) are conveyed through **two entirely separate vertical stacks**, each provided with its **own vent pipe** extending above the roof terrace.

- The **soil stack** (100 mm dia) receives discharge from WCs only. It connects to the **manhole** at the bottom and to a **vent pipe** at the top.
- The **waste stack** (50 mm dia) receives discharge from washbasins, baths, and kitchen sinks through floor traps. It also connects to its own **vent pipe** at the top and to a separate **gully trap / inspection chamber** at the bottom.
- The two stacks are completely isolated to prevent cross-contamination of foul gases.

**Neat Sketch (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A2[ROOF] -->|VENT 50MM| B2[SOIL VENT]
    A2[ROOF] -->|VENT 50MM| C2[WASTE VENT]
    D2[WC] -->|SOIL 100MM| E2[SOIL STACK 100MM]
    E2[SOIL STACK 100MM] --> F2[MANHOLE]
    E2[SOIL STACK 100MM] --> B2[SOIL VENT]
    G2[WASH BASIN] -->|WASTE 40MM| H2[WASTE STACK 50MM]
    I2[BATH] -->|WASTE 40MM| H2[WASTE STACK 50MM]
    J2[KITCHEN SINK] -->|WASTE 50MM| H2[WASTE STACK 50MM]
    H2[WASTE STACK 50MM] --> K2[GULLY TRAP]
    H2[WASTE STACK 50MM] --> C2[WASTE VENT]
    K2[GULLY TRAP] --> L2[INSPECTION CHAMBER]
    F2[MANHOLE] --> M2[SEPTIC TANK]
    L2[INSPECTION CHAMBER] --> M2[SEPTIC TANK]
```

**Four Advantages of Two-Pipe System:**
1. **Most hygienic** — soil and waste are completely separated, eliminating cross-flow of foul gases.
2. **No siphonage** — every stack is independently vented, protecting trap seals.
3. **Independent maintenance** — soil and waste stacks can be cleaned without affecting each other.
4. **Suitable for high-rise buildings, hospitals, and hotels** where reliability is critical.

**Two Disadvantages of Two-Pipe System:**
1. **Highest cost** — more pipe length, more fittings, more labour.
2. **Complex routing** — requires more space in walls and shafts; clashes with structural members are more likely.

---

#### Question B. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`  **(INTERNAL CHOICE)**

**(a)** A residential building has the following sanitary fixtures:
  - 3 Water Closets (WC) — each with flushing cistern
  - 2 Wash Basins
  - 2 Bath Tubs
  - 1 Kitchen Sink
  - 1 Urinal

  Using the **Hunter's Fixture Unit method**, determine the **design discharge** and **size the building drain**, assuming the drain is laid at a gradient of **1 in 100** with Manning's $n = 0.015$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **working of an intercepting trap** and the **principle of venting** in a soil stack. Why is the vent pipe extended **900 mm above the roof terrace**? **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (Question B):**

##### Part (a) — Fixture Unit Method [7 marks]

**Step 1: Assign Fixture Unit values (from IS 5329 / Hunter's Table)** [2 marks]

| Fixture | Quantity | Unit weight (FU) | Total FU |
|---|---|---|---|
| WC with cistern | 3 | 5 | 15 |
| Wash Basin | 2 | 1 | 2 |
| Bath Tub | 2 | 2 | 4 |
| Kitchen Sink | 1 | 2 | 2 |
| Urinal | 1 | 3 | 3 |
| **Total** | | | **26 FU** |

**Step 2: Convert FU to Design Discharge** [2 marks]

From standard Hunter's curves (probable simultaneous use 100%), 26 FU ≈ **2.5 L/s** design discharge.

$$Q_{design} = 2.5 \text{ L/s} = 0.0025 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Assume a trial diameter and find velocity** [1 mark]

Try $D = 0.100$ m (100 mm), $S = 1/100 = 0.01$, $n = 0.015$, $R_h = D/4 = 0.025$ m.

$$V = \frac{1}{0.015} \times (0.025)^{2/3} \times (0.01)^{1/2}$$

$$V = 66.667 \times 0.0855 \times 0.1 = 0.570 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Check capacity and self-cleansing velocity** [1 mark]

$$Q_{capacity} = A \times V = 0.007854 \times 0.570 = 0.00448 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 4.48 \text{ L/s}$$

Since $Q_{capacity} = 4.48$ L/s $> Q_{design} = 2.5$ L/s, the 100 mm pipe is **adequate in capacity**.

However, $V = 0.570$ m/s $< 0.6$ m/s, so the self-cleansing condition is **NOT satisfied** in a 100 mm pipe at 1 in 100 gradient.

**Step 5: Re-design by either increasing diameter or gradient** [1 mark]

**Option A**: Increase $D$ to 150 mm. New $R_h = 0.150/4 = 0.0375$ m.

$$V = \frac{1}{0.015} \times (0.0375)^{2/3} \times (0.01)^{1/2} = 66.667 \times 0.1119 \times 0.1 = 0.7458 \text{ m/s}$$

This is $> 0.6$ m/s. **Adopt 150 mm dia at 1 in 100.** ✅

**Final Answer**: Design the building drain as **150 mm diameter** at a gradient of **1 in 100**.

---

##### Part (b) — Intercepting Trap and Venting [7 marks]

**Intercepting Trap — Description (3 marks):**

An **intercepting trap** is a U-shaped trap (similar to a gully trap but larger, typically 100–150 mm) installed at the **last manhole** of the building's external sewer line, just before the connection to the **municipal sewer or septic tank**. It contains a **water seal of 100 mm** (deeper than a gully trap).

**Working**: It intercepts the sewer gases rising from the municipal sewer and prevents them from travelling back through the building's internal drain. The deep water seal ensures that even a small pressure fluctuation does not break the seal. It also has a **rodding eye (cleanout plug)** at the top for desilting.

**Neat Sketch of Intercepting Trap (using Mermaid block flow):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A3[BUILDING DRAIN] --> B3[INCOMING 100MM]
    B3[INCOMING 100MM] --> C3[U BEND WATER SEAL 100MM]
    C3[U BEND WATER SEAL 100MM] --> D3[OUTGOING TO SEWER]
    D3[OUTGOING TO SEWER] --> E3[MUNICIPAL SEWER]
    C3[U BEND WATER SEAL 100MM] --> F3[RODDING EYE AT TOP]
    C3[U BEND WATER SEAL 100MM] --> G3[VENT TO MANHOLE COVER]
```

**Principle of Venting in a Soil Stack (2 marks):**

When a column of water falls down a soil stack, it creates a **negative pressure (vacuum)** behind it. This vacuum, if unchecked, **siphons the water seals** of traps connected to branch pipes. The **vent pipe** (50 mm dia) running parallel to the soil stack and extending above the roof admits **atmospheric air** into the stack, breaking the vacuum and **equalising the pressure** to atmospheric. This protects all trap seals.

**Why vent extended 900 mm above the roof (2 marks):**

As per **NBC 2016, Part 9, Section 5.6.2**, the vent pipe must extend at least **900 mm above the roof terrace** (or 600 mm above the nearest window within 3 m horizontal distance) for three reasons:
1. To **disperse foul gases** above the breathing zone of occupants on the terrace.
2. To **prevent downdraft** of sewer gases back into the building through windows or ventilators.
3. To comply with the statutory **safety distance** from openings as required by the **IS 5329:1983** code.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in Manning's equation → wrong answer by a factor of 1000. (–3 marks)
> 2. **Confusing soil stack and waste stack** in the two-pipe system diagram → examiner deducts for ambiguous labeling. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Missing the 900 mm vent extension** dimension in the sketch → –2 marks.
> 4. **Not verifying the self-cleansing velocity** in numerical problems → –2 marks (it is a compulsory final step).
> 5. **Drawing floor trap and gully trap as the same symbol** → –1 mark. (Floor trap has a grating, gully trap has a masonry chamber with cover.)
> 6. **Forgetting the rodding eye / cleanout plug** at the upstream end of every horizontal drain → –1 mark.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 Experiment 2**

- ✅ A **plumbing drawing** shows the **complete pipe network** of a building (water supply + drainage + venting).
- ✅ A **sanitary drawing** is a **subset** that focuses on **soil, waste, vent, and disposal**.
- ✅ The **four standard systems** of plumbing are: **Single Stack, One-Pipe, One-Pipe Partially Ventilated, Two-Pipe** — remember their order by complexity and cost.
- ✅ The **two-pipe system** is the **most hygienic** and the **most expensive** — preferred for hospitals and high-rise.
- ✅ **Self-cleansing velocity** must lie between **0.6 m/s (minimum)** and **3.0 m/s (maximum)** — this is the single most-tested numerical condition.
- ✅ **Manning's equation** $V = (1/n) \cdot R_h^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ is the workhorse for **gravity drain** design.
- ✅ **Hazen-Williams** is used for **pressurised water supply** pipes, not for sewers.
- ✅ **Minimum gradient for 100 mm drain = 1 in 60**; for **150 mm = 1 in 100**; for **225 mm = 1 in 120 to 1 in 200** (per IS 1742).
- ✅ **Trap seal depth ≥ 50 mm** to prevent siphonage of foul gases into the building.
- ✅ **Vent pipe extension above roof ≥ 900 mm** (NBC 2016, IS 5329).
- ✅ **Gully trap** is at the **building face** before the inspection chamber; **floor trap** is **inside** the bathroom/kitchen.
- ✅ **Manhole** is for **deep junctions** (depth $> 1.0$ m); **inspection chamber** is for **shallow junctions** (depth $\leq 1.0$ m).
- ✅ **Septic tank** is designed for **5–10 years** desludging interval; sized as per **NBC 2016** for a given number of users.
- ✅ **Drawing scales**: Plan = **1:50 / 1:100**; Sectional elevation = **1:20 / 1:10**; Isometric schematic = **not to scale**.
- ✅ **Standard pipe diameters to memorise**: 15, 20, 25, 40, 50, 75, 100, 150 mm.
- ✅ Always **label pipe diameters, gradients, materials (PVC / CI / GI / stoneware), and direction of flow** on the drawing — examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for proper **annotation discipline**.
- ✅ **Soak pit** is used when no municipal sewer is available; it is a **percolation pit** of rubble/brick, minimum 1.5 m deep and 1 m diameter for a small house.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plan and prepare electrical drawings of a building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL507 - CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Plan and prepare electrical drawings of a building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrical Drawing of a Building** is a specialized set of technical drawings that graphically represent the complete electrical installation system of a residential, commercial, or industrial building. These drawings follow the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** specifications, primarily **IS 732 (1989)** – *Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations* and **IS 375 (1987)** – *Symbols and Abbreviations for use in Electrical and Electronic Engineering Drawings*.

The drawing set typically includes:
- **Electrical Layout Plan** – Shows positions of switches, sockets, light points, fan points, and appliances on the building plan.
- **Single Line Diagram (SLD)** – Represents the distribution network from the utility supply to the final sub-circuits.
- **Wiring Diagram** – Details the physical connections between conductors and accessories.
- **Load Schedule** – Tabulates connected load, maximum demand, and diversity factor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of an electrical drawing as the "circulatory system map" of a building.** Just as a doctor needs an anatomical chart to trace blood flow through veins and arteries, an electrical engineer needs a drawing to trace current flow through wires, switches, and loads. The **service line** is the "main artery" from the power grid, the **distribution board (DB)** acts as the "heart" that pumps and regulates power, the **switches** are "valves" controlling flow, and the **loads (lights, fans)** are the "organs" that consume energy.

## 1.3 Governing Standards and Key Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Standards for KTU Examination:**
> - **IS 732:1989** – Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations
> - **IS 375:1987** – Electrical Symbols and Abbreviations
> - **IS 4648:1968** – Guide for Electrical Layout in Residential Buildings
> - **National Electrical Code (NEC) 2011**
> - **IE Rules 1956** – Indian Electricity Rules

| Standard Parameter | Specified Value |
|---|---|
| Standard Domestic Supply Voltage | **230 V, Single Phase, 50 Hz AC** |
| Standard Industrial Supply | **415 V, Three Phase, 50 Hz AC** |
| Maximum Load per Sub-circuit (Lighting) | **800 W** (as per IS 732) |
| Maximum Points per Sub-circuit | **10 points** (for lighting) |
| Minimum Height of Switch Board | **1.25 m** above floor level |
| Standard Ceiling Fan Point Height | **2.75 m to 3.0 m** above floor |
| Socket Outlet Height (General) | **0.3 m** above floor |
| Kitchen Socket Height | **1.2 m** above working platform |
| Minimum Size of Earth Continuity Conductor (Copper) | **1.5 mm²** (for 1.5 mm² wiring) |
| Standard Lamp Power Ratings (Domestic) | **40W, 60W, 100W, LED equivalents** |
| Standard Ceiling Fan Power | **60W to 75W** |
| Diversity Factor for Residential | **0.6 to 0.8** |
| Power Factor (Domestic) | **0.8 lagging (assumed)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must know how to prepare (i) Electrical layout plan, (ii) Single line diagram, and (iii) Wiring diagram using standard BIS symbols on the given building plan.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Electrical Drawings

### A. Electrical Layout Plan (Floor-wise)
A plan view drawing showing the **location** of every electrical accessory on a scaled floor plan (typically 1:50 or 1:100 scale). It includes:
- Position of ceiling fans, light points, wall brackets
- Switch boards and their controlling switches
- Socket outlets (5A and 15A)
- Main distribution board (MDB) and sub-distribution boards (SDB)
- Routing of conduit/trunking (sometimes shown separately)
- Call-out/circuit numbers

### B. Single Line Diagram (SLD)
A symbolic representation where each conductor of a multi-phase system is shown as a **single line**. It depicts:
- Incoming supply (HT/LT)
- Energy meter
- Main switch (MCB/Isolator)
- Distribution board with MCBs/MCCBs
- Sub-circuits with protective devices
- Earthing system
- Load distribution

### C. Wiring Diagram (Schematic)
Shows the **electrical connections** between components using standard symbols, including:
- Phase (L), Neutral (N), Earth (E) conductors
- Switch-leg wires
- Loop-in, loop-out connections
- Junction boxes and their connections

### D. Load Schedule / Schedule of Works
A tabulated statement listing:
- Circuit number
- Description of load
- Number of points
- Wattage per point
- Total wattage
- Type and size of cable
- MCB rating

## 2.2 Components of Building Electrical System

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Power Distribution (Domestic Building):**
> 1. **Electricity Board Service Line** → 2. **Energy Meter** → 3. **Main Switch / MCB (Double Pole)** → 4. **Distribution Board (DB)** → 5. **Sub-circuits (Lighting / Power)** → 6. **Final Sub-circuits** → 7. **Loads (Points)**

### Major Components:

1. **Service Connection** – From utility pole/transformer to building, terminated at the **service cable entry point**.
2. **Energy Meter** – Records total energy consumed; placed in a meter box.
3. **Main Switch / Isolator** – Master control to cut off entire building supply; usually 60A–100A DP (Double Pole) MCB.
4. **Distribution Board (DB)** – Houses MCBs/MCBs for individual sub-circuits. Two types: **Single Phase DB** (SPN) and **Three Phase DB** (TPN).
5. **MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker)** – Protective device rated in amperes; typical ratings: **6A, 10A, 16A, 20A, 32A, 40A, 63A**.
6. **Earthing System** – Three types: **Pipe Earthing, Plate Earthing, Rod Earthing**. Domestic buildings use **GI pipe electrode of 40 mm diameter, 2.75 m deep** with alternating layers of charcoal and salt.
7. **Conductors** – Phase (Red), Neutral (Black), Earth (Green) – color-coded as per IS.

## 2.3 BIS Standard Electrical Symbols (IS 375)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These symbols MUST be memorized for KTU board examination.**

| Component | BIS Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Ceiling Fan | ⌀ with 4 lines | Three-speed regulator type |
| Ceiling Light Point | × or filled circle | Junction with ceiling rose |
| Wall Bracket Light | —◊— or half-arrow | Flush mount on wall |
| 5A Socket Outlet | ⊙ or rectangle with dot | 3-pin 5-ampere outlet |
| 15A Socket Outlet | ⊙ with cross or larger rect | 3-pin 15-ampere outlet (AC/Power) |
| Single Pole Switch (SPST) | ▱ with diagonal | One-way switch |
| Two-Way Switch | ▱ with double diagonal | Used in staircases |
| Bell Push | ▱ with dot | Doorbell activator |
| MCB | ▭ with notch | Miniature Circuit Breaker |
| Main Switch (DP) | ▭ with double line | Double Pole isolator |
| Distribution Board | ▭ with subdivisions | DB housing MCBs |
| Energy Meter | ⌬ or circle with M | kWh meter |
| Earth Point | ⏚ (three horizontal lines) | Earth electrode/connection |
| Ceiling Rose | ⊗ or circle with X | Connection point for pendant |
| Exhaust Fan | ☐ with ↻ arrow | Wall/ceiling mounted fan |
| Call Bell | ⊙ with waves | Doorbell indicator |
| Fluorescent Tube | —▭— | Tube light with choke |

## 2.4 Wiring Systems Used in Buildings

> [!NOTE]
> **As per IS 732, the following wiring systems are recommended:**

| Wiring System | Application | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Concealed Conduit (PVC)** | Modern residential/commercial | Most common; concealed in walls/slabs |
| **Surface Conduit (PVC/MS)** | Industrial, retrofit | Mounted on wall surface |
| **Batten Wiring (CTS/PVC)** | Temporary, old buildings | Cables clipped to wooden battens |
| **Lead Sheathed Wiring** | Obsolete | Hazardous; rarely used |
| **Cleat Wiring** | Temporary installations | Cables on porcelain cleats |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to specify the **type of wiring** (concealed/surface conduit) and **size of conduit** in their drawings. Always mention it in the notes/specifications.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in Part A and Part B questions regularly.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = V \times I \times \cos\phi$ | Single-phase AC power | W |
| 2 | $P = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi$ | Three-phase AC power | W |
| 3 | $E = P \times t$ | Energy consumed (kWh = Units) | kWh |
| 4 | $\text{Maximum Demand} = \text{Connected Load} \times \text{Diversity Factor}$ | Peak load estimation | W or kW |
| 5 | $\text{Total Load (W)} = \sum (n_i \times W_i)$ | Sum of (number of points × wattage) | W |
| 6 | $I = \dfrac{P}{V \times \cos\phi}$ | Current calculation | A |
| 7 | $\text{MCB Rating} = 1.25 \times I_{\text{load}}$ | Safety margin for circuit breaker | A |
| 8 | $\text{No. of Sub-circuits} = \dfrac{\text{Total Load}}{\text{Max Load per Circuit}}$ | For 230V, lighting = 800W max | – |
| 9 | $\text{Voltage Drop} = I \times R \times L \times 2$ (loop) | Loop length for single-phase | V |
| 10 | $R = \dfrac{\rho \times L}{A}$ | Resistance of conductor | Ω |
| 11 | $\text{Current per Phase} = \dfrac{\text{Total Load}}{3 \times V_{\text{phase}} \times \cos\phi}$ | Three-phase balance | A |
| 12 | $\text{Earthing Resistance} < 5 \, \Omega$ | For domestic installations | Ω |
| 13 | $\text{Conduit Fill} = \dfrac{\text{Total CSA of Cables}}{\text{CSA of Conduit}} \leq 40\%$ | As per IS 732 | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Diversity Factor values to remember:**
> - Residential: **0.5 to 0.7**
> - Commercial (Office): **0.7 to 0.8**
> - Industrial: **0.8 to 0.9**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: How to Plan Electrical Drawing (Step-by-Step Procedure)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the KTU Board Exam evaluation checklist — follow it strictly.**

### Step 1: Study the Building Plan
- Identify **rooms and their functions** (bedroom, kitchen, bathroom, hall, etc.)
- Note **door/window positions** to plan switch board locations
- Determine **load centers** (kitchen has high load, bedrooms have lighting/fan load)

### Step 2: Decide Number of Points
Apply IS 4648 guidelines:
- **Bedroom**: 1 light, 1 fan, 1–2 sockets (5A), 1 AC point (15A)
- **Living Room**: 1–2 lights, 1 fan, 2–3 sockets, 1 TV point
- **Kitchen**: 1 light, 1 exhaust fan, 2–3 sockets (5A for mixie), 1 socket (15A for fridge/geyser)
- **Bathroom**: 1 light (wall bracket), 1 exhaust fan, 1 geyser point (15A)
- **Verandah/Balcony**: 1 light, 1 socket (optional)
- **Staircase**: 1 light per floor, controlled by 2-way switches

### Step 3: Locate Switch Boards
- **Inside the room** when entering
- **Height: 1.25 m** above floor level
- Group switches on common board (1.0 m × 1.5 m board)

### Step 4: Calculate Connected Load
Sum up wattage of all points on each sub-circuit.

### Step 5: Design Sub-circuits
**Maximum 800 W** per lighting sub-circuit, **Maximum 3000 W** per power sub-circuit.

### Step 6: Select MCB Ratings
$I_{\text{load}} = \dfrac{P}{V \times \cos\phi}$; MCB Rating = $1.25 \times I_{\text{load}}$

### Step 7: Draw Single Line Diagram
### Step 8: Draw Layout Plan
### Step 9: Prepare Load Schedule

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Load Calculation for a Residential Building

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the most frequently asked question type in KTU 2024 Scheme — 14 marks.**

### Problem Statement:
A residential building has the following rooms with the indicated electrical points. **Design the electrical layout, calculate the total connected load, determine the number of sub-circuits, and size the main switch.**

| Room | Light Points | Fan Points | 5A Sockets | 15A Sockets |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Drawing Room | 2 (100W each) | 1 (75W) | 3 (100W) | 1 AC (1500W) |
| Bedroom 1 | 1 (100W) | 1 (75W) | 2 (100W) | – |
| Bedroom 2 | 1 (100W) | 1 (75W) | 2 (100W) | 1 Geyser (2000W) |
| Kitchen | 1 (100W) | 1 (75W exhaust) | 3 (100W) | 1 (1000W) |
| Bathroom | 1 (60W) | 1 (40W exhaust) | – | – |
| Verandah | 1 (60W) | – | 1 (100W) | – |
| Staircase | 1 (60W) | – | – | – |

### Step-by-Step Solution:

#### Step 1: Calculate Lighting Load

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Drawing Room} &= 2 \times 100 = 200 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Bedroom 1} &= 1 \times 100 = 100 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Bedroom 2} &= 1 \times 100 = 100 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Kitchen} &= 1 \times 100 = 100 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Bathroom} &= 1 \times 60 = 60 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Verandah} &= 1 \times 60 = 60 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Staircase} &= 1 \times 60 = 60 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Total Lighting Load} &= 200 + 100 + 100 + 100 + 60 + 60 + 60 = 680 \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2: Calculate Fan Load

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Drawing Room} &= 1 \times 75 = 75 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Bedroom 1} &= 1 \times 75 = 75 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Bedroom 2} &= 1 \times 75 = 75 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Kitchen (Exhaust)} &= 1 \times 75 = 75 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Bathroom (Exhaust)} &= 1 \times 40 = 40 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Total Fan Load} &= 75 + 75 + 75 + 75 + 40 = 340 \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Calculate 5A Socket Load (assuming 100W per socket)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Drawing Room} &= 3 \times 100 = 300 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Bedroom 1} &= 2 \times 100 = 200 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Bedroom 2} &= 2 \times 100 = 200 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Kitchen} &= 3 \times 100 = 300 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Verandah} &= 1 \times 100 = 100 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Total 5A Socket Load} &= 300 + 200 + 200 + 300 + 100 = 1100 \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Calculate 15A Socket Load (Power Load)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AC (Drawing Room)} &= 1500 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Geyser (Bedroom 2)} &= 2000 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Kitchen (Heavy duty)} &= 1000 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Total 15A Power Load} &= 1500 + 2000 + 1000 = 4500 \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Calculate Total Connected Load

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= P_{\text{light}} + P_{\text{fan}} + P_{\text{socket 5A}} + P_{\text{power 15A}} \\
P_{\text{total}} &= 680 + 340 + 1100 + 4500 \\
P_{\text{total}} &= 6620 \, \text{W} = 6.62 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6: Apply Diversity Factor (Take 0.6 for residential)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{max demand}} &= P_{\text{connected}} \times \text{DF} \\
P_{\text{max demand}} &= 6620 \times 0.6 \\
P_{\text{max demand}} &= 3972 \, \text{W} \approx 3.97 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7: Calculate Main Current

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{main}} &= \frac{P_{\text{max}}}{V \times \cos\phi} \\
&= \frac{3972}{230 \times 0.8} \\
&= \frac{3972}{184} \\
I_{\text{main}} &= 21.59 \, \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Next higher standard MCB/Main Switch rating = 32 A DP MCB**

#### Step 8: Determine Number of Sub-circuits

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lighting + Fan Sub-circuits} &= \frac{P_{\text{light}} + P_{\text{fan}}}{800} = \frac{680 + 340}{800} = 1.275 \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Use 2 lighting sub-circuits} \\
\text{Power Sub-circuits} &= \frac{P_{\text{power}}}{3000} = \frac{4500}{3000} = 1.5 \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Use 2 power sub-circuits} \\
\text{Socket (5A) Sub-circuits} &= \frac{P_{\text{5A}}}{800} = \frac{1100}{800} = 1.375 \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Use 2 socket sub-circuits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Sub-circuits = 6**

#### Step 9: Sizing MCBs for Each Sub-circuit

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lighting circuit current} &= \frac{510 \, \text{W (avg per circuit)}}{230 \times 0.8} = 2.77 \, \text{A} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{6 A MCB} \\
\text{Power circuit (15A) current} &= \frac{2250 \, \text{W (avg)}}{230 \times 0.8} = 12.22 \, \text{A} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{16 A MCB} \\
\text{5A socket circuit current} &= \frac{550 \, \text{W (avg)}}{230 \times 0.8} = 2.99 \, \text{A} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{6 A MCB}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 10: Final Single Line Diagram Specification

> [!NOTE]
> **SLD Flow:**  
> **Pole → Service Cable → Energy Meter → 32A DP MCB (Main Switch) → Distribution Board with 6 MCBs → 6 Sub-circuits → Loads**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Conduit Size Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Important for Part A 3-mark questions.**

A sub-circuit has 3 cables of 2.5 mm² (CSA = 4.92 mm² each) and 1 cable of 1.5 mm² (CSA = 2.41 mm²). Find the suitable PVC conduit size.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total cable CSA} &= (3 \times 4.92) + 2.41 = 14.76 + 2.41 = 17.17 \, \text{mm}^2 \\
\text{Maximum allowable fill} &= 40\% \\
\text{Conduit internal area required} &= \frac{17.17}{0.40} = 42.93 \, \text{mm}^2 \\
\text{Conduit internal diameter required} &= \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 42.93}{\pi}} = 7.39 \, \text{mm} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Use 20 mm PVC conduit (standard)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Voltage Drop Calculation (KTU Expected)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage drop should not exceed 3% of supply voltage from DB to furthest point (as per IS 732).**

For a 230V single-phase sub-circuit with current $I$ flowing through a cable of length $L$ and resistance $R$ per km:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Loop length} &= 2L \text{ (Phase + Neutral)} \\
V_{\text{drop}} &= I \times 2L \times R \\
V_{\text{drop \%}} &= \frac{V_{\text{drop}}}{230} \times 100 \leq 3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resistivity of Copper:** $\rho = 0.0172 \, \Omega \cdot \text{mm}^2/\text{m}$

---

## 3.5 Earthing Resistance Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **For pipe earthing (most common domestic):**

$$
R_e = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left( \frac{4L}{d} \right) - 1 \right]
$$

Where:
- $R_e$ = Earth electrode resistance (Ω)
- $\rho$ = Soil resistivity (Ω·m); **typical = 100 Ω·m** for moist soil
- $L$ = Length of electrode (m); typically **2.75 m**
- $d$ = Diameter of electrode (m); typically **0.040 m** (40 mm)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_e &= \frac{100}{2 \pi \times 2.75} \left[ \ln\left( \frac{4 \times 2.75}{0.040} \right) - 1 \right] \\
&= 5.787 \left[ \ln(275) - 1 \right] \\
&= 5.787 \left[ 5.617 - 1 \right] \\
R_e &= 5.787 \times 4.617 = 26.72 \, \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

To reduce this below **5 Ω**, multiple electrodes are connected in parallel, or charcoal/salt backfilling is used.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single Line Diagram (SLD) — Standard for KTU Board Exam

> [!NOTE]
> **The following Mermaid block renders a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Single Line Diagram. This is the correct adaptation for a Mermaid-renderable schematic since physical SLDs require specialized CAD.**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pole["UTILITY POLE OR TRANSFORMER"]:::source
    service["SERVICE CABLE 11KV OR 230V"]:::line
    meter["ENERGY METER KWH"]:::meter
    mainMCB["MAIN SWITCH 32A DP MCB"]:::main
    db["DISTRIBUTION BOARD DB"]:::dbnode
    mcb1["6A MCB LIGHTING CIRCUIT 1"]:::submc
    mcb2["6A MCB LIGHTING CIRCUIT 2"]:::submc
    mcb3["6A MCB SOCKET CIRCUIT 1"]:::submc
    mcb4["6A MCB SOCKET CIRCUIT 2"]:::submc
    mcb5["16A MCB POWER AC GEYSER"]:::submc
    mcb6["16A MCB POWER KITCHEN"]:::submc
    earth["EARTHING SYSTEM PIPE OR PLATE"]:::earth
    light1["LIGHT POINTS LAMPS"]:::load
    light2["FAN POINTS REGULATOR"]:::load
    socket5["5A SOCKETS GENERAL"]:::load
    socket15["15A SOCKETS POWER APPLIANCES"]:::load

    pole -->|HT LINE| service
    service --> meter
    meter --> mainMCB
    mainMCB --> db
    db --> mcb1
    db --> mcb2
    db --> mcb3
    db --> mcb4
    db --> mcb5
    db --> mcb6
    mcb1 --> light1
    mcb2 --> light2
    mcb3 --> socket5
    mcb4 --> socket5
    mcb5 --> socket15
    mcb6 --> socket15
    mainMCB -->|EARTH CONDUCTOR| earth

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef line fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef meter fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef main fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dbnode fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef submc fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef earth fill:#8B4513,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef load fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow of Electrical Layout Planning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start(["START: RECEIVE BUILDING PLAN"]):::step1
    study["STUDY ROOMS LAYOUT"]:::step2
    list["LIST ELECTRICAL POINTS PER ROOM"]:::step3
    locate["LOCATE SWITCH BOARDS AT 1.25M"]:::step4
    mark["MARK POINTS ON FLOOR PLAN"]:::step5
    sub["DIVIDE POINTS INTO SUB CIRCUITS MAX 800W"]:::step6
    mcb["SELECT MCB RATINGS PER CIRCUIT"]:::step7
    sld["PREPARE SINGLE LINE DIAGRAM"]:::step8
    layout["FINALIZE LAYOUT PLAN WITH SYMBOLS"]:::step9
    sched["PREPARE LOAD SCHEDULE TABLE"]:::step10
    earth["DESIGN EARTHING LAYOUT"]:::step11
    complete(["SUBMIT DRAWING SET"]):::done

    start --> study --> list --> locate --> mark --> sub --> mcb --> sld --> layout --> sched --> earth --> complete

    classDef step1 fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step2 fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step3 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step4 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step5 fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#1A237E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step6 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step7 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step8 fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#004D40,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step9 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step10 fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step11 fill:#EFEBE9,stroke:#3E2723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef done fill:#76FF03,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Wiring System Hierarchy in a Building

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    main["MAIN SUPPLY 230V 50Hz"]:::top
    M["METER BOX ENERGY METER"]:::box1
    MS["MAIN SWITCH 32A DP"]:::box2
    DBM["DISTRIBUTION BOARD MDB"]:::box3
    C1["CIRCUIT 1 LIGHTING 6A MCB"]:::box4
    C2["CIRCUIT 2 LIGHTING 6A MCB"]:::box4
    C3["CIRCUIT 3 5A SOCKETS 6A MCB"]:::box4
    C4["CIRCUIT 4 5A SOCKETS 6A MCB"]:::box4
    C5["CIRCUIT 5 POWER 16A MCB"]:::box4
    C6["CIRCUIT 6 POWER 16A MCB"]:::box4
    L1["LOOP IN CEILING ROSE LIGHT 1"]:::load1
    L2["LOOP IN CEILING ROSE LIGHT 2"]:::load1
    F1["FAN WITH REGULATOR"]:::load2
    S1["5A SOCKET PHASE NEUTRAL EARTH"]:::load3
    P1["15A SOCKET AC GEYSER"]:::load4
    E["EARTHING PIPE 2.75M DEEP"]:::earth

    main --> M --> MS --> DBM
    DBM --> C1 --> L1
    DBM --> C2 --> L2
    DBM --> C2 --> F1
    DBM --> C3 --> S1
    DBM --> C4 --> S1
    DBM --> C5 --> P1
    DBM --> C6 --> P1
    DBM -->|EARTH WIRE GREEN| E
    L1 --> E
    L2 --> E
    S1 --> E
    P1 --> E

    classDef top fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef box1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef box2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef box3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef box4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load1 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load2 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load3 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load4 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef earth fill:#8B4513,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

## 4.4 Schematic Diagram: Loop-in-Loop-out Connection for Light Points

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the most common wiring pattern in residential buildings. The phase wire loops at each ceiling rose using a connector block; switches receive the switched-phase wire from the switchboard.**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    db["DB PHASE AND NEUTRAL"]:::top
    sw["SWITCH BOARD S1 S2 S3"]:::switch
    cr1["CEILING ROSE 1"]:::rose
    cr2["CEILING ROSE 2"]:::rose
    cr3["CEILING ROSE 3"]:::rose
    lamp1["LAMP 1"]:::lamp
    lamp2["LAMP 2"]:::lamp
    lamp3["LAMP 3"]:::lamp
    n["NEUTRAL LINK"]:::neutral

    db -->|PHASE L| sw
    sw -->|SWITCHED PHASE| cr1
    sw -->|SWITCHED PHASE| cr2
    sw -->|SWITCHED PHASE| cr3
    db -->|NEUTRAL N| n
    n --> cr1
    n --> cr2
    n --> cr3
    cr1 --> lamp1
    cr2 --> lamp2
    cr3 --> lamp3

    classDef top fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef switch fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rose fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lamp fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef neutral fill:#000000,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any six standard BIS electrical symbols used in building wiring drawings with their meaning.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | ⏚ (Three horizontal lines, decreasing) | Earth Point / Earthing |
| 2 | ⊗ (Circle with X) | Ceiling Rose / Light Point |
| 3 | ↻ (Circle with curved arrow) | Ceiling Fan / Exhaust Fan |
| 4 | ▭ with diagonal | Single Pole One-Way Switch (SPST) |
| 5 | ⊙ (Circle with dot) | 5A Socket Outlet (3-pin) |
| 6 | ▭ with notch | MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker) |
| 7 | ⌬ (Circle with letter M) | Energy Meter |
| 8 | ▭ with double line | Double Pole Main Switch (DP) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always draw the symbols neatly using a stencil; a freehand symbol loses 0.5 to 1 mark.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the standard supply voltage, frequency, and the maximum number of points allowed in a single lighting sub-circuit as per IS 732.**

**Model Answer:**

As per **IS 732:1989 (Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations)**:
- **Standard supply voltage:** **230 V, Single Phase, AC**
- **Standard frequency:** **50 Hz**
- **Maximum load per lighting sub-circuit:** **800 W**
- **Maximum number of points per sub-circuit:** **10 points** (each point ≤ 100 W)
- **Maximum load per power sub-circuit:** **3000 W**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Memorize the 800W / 10-point rule — it is a guaranteed question in some form every year.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions carry internal choice — students must attempt one of the two options.**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**A residential building has the following electrical loads:**

| Location | Lights (60W) | Fans (75W) | 5A Sockets (100W each) | 15A Sockets |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hall | 2 | 1 | 2 | 1 (1500W AC) |
| Bedroom 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | – |
| Bedroom 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 (2000W Geyser) |
| Kitchen | 1 | 1 exhaust | 2 | 1 (1000W) |
| Toilet | 1 | 1 exhaust | – | – |
| Verandah | 1 | – | 1 | – |

**(a)** Calculate the total connected load and maximum demand. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Draw the Single Line Diagram (SLD) and determine MCB ratings. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

### Part (a): Load Calculation

**Step 1 — Total Lighting Load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lights} &= (2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1) \times 60 \\
&= 7 \times 60 = 420 \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Fan Load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fans} &= (1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1) \times 75 \\
&= 5 \times 75 = 375 \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total 5A Socket Load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{5A Sockets} &= (2 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 0 + 1) \times 100 \\
&= 7 \times 100 = 700 \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total 15A Power Load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{15A Sockets} &= 1500 + 2000 + 1000 = 4500 \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Connected Load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{connected}} &= 420 + 375 + 700 + 4500 \\
&= 5995 \, \text{W} \approx 6.0 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Demand (DF = 0.6 for residential):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{max demand}} &= 5995 \times 0.6 \\
&= 3597 \, \text{W} \approx 3.6 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Main Current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{main}} &= \frac{P_{\text{max}}}{V \times \cos\phi} = \frac{3597}{230 \times 0.8} \\
&= 19.55 \, \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Main MCB rating = 32 A DP (next higher standard size).**

---

### Part (b): Single Line Diagram and MCB Ratings

**Number of Sub-circuits:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lighting + Fan Sub-circuits} &= \frac{420 + 375}{800} = 0.99 \approx 1 \\
&\text{Use 1 lighting sub-circuit (well within limit)} \\
\text{Power Sub-circuits} &= \frac{4500}{3000} = 1.5 \Rightarrow 2 \text{ circuits} \\
\text{5A Socket Sub-circuits} &= \frac{700}{800} = 0.875 \approx 1 \text{ circuit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Sub-circuits = 4**

**MCB Rating per Sub-circuit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lighting} &: \frac{795 \, \text{W}}{230 \times 0.8} = 4.32 \, \text{A} \Rightarrow \mathbf{6 \, A} \, \text{MCB} \\
\text{Power Circuit 1 (AC + Geyser)} &: \frac{3500}{184} = 19.02 \, \text{A} \Rightarrow \mathbf{20 \, A} \, \text{MCB} \\
\text{Power Circuit 2 (Kitchen)} &: \frac{1000}{184} = 5.43 \, \text{A} \Rightarrow \mathbf{6 \, A} \, \text{MCB} \\
\text{5A Socket Circuit} &: \frac{700}{184} = 3.80 \, \text{A} \Rightarrow \mathbf{6 \, A} \, \text{MCB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final SLD Specification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Pole} \to \text{Service Cable} \to \text{Energy Meter} \to \text{32A DP MCB} \to \\
&\text{Distribution Board} \to \begin{cases}
6A \text{ MCB} \to \text{Lighting + Fan} \\
20A \text{ MCB} \to \text{AC + Geyser} \\
6A \text{ MCB} \to \text{Kitchen Power} \\
6A \text{ MCB} \to \text{5A Sockets}
\end{cases} \\
&\text{Earthing: GI Pipe 40mm dia, 2.75m deep}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the various types of wiring systems used in residential buildings with their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** What are the BIS standards applicable to building electrical drawings? List at least 4 important symbols with their meaning. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

### Part (a): Types of Wiring Systems

| Wiring System | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cleat Wiring** | Cables on porcelain cleats fixed on walls | Cheapest, easy to inspect, easy to install | Not durable, not suitable for damp areas, not aesthetic |
| **Batten Wiring** | Cables run on teakwood/PVC battens with clips | Simple, neat appearance, easily inspectable | Not suitable for outdoor, can sag over time |
| **Casing-Capping** | Cables enclosed in wooden/PVC casing | Protected, neat, durable | Costly, requires skilled labor |
| **Conduit Wiring – Surface** | PVC/MS conduit on wall surface | Mechanically strong, easy to inspect, durable | Less aesthetic, conduit visible |
| **Conduit Wiring – Concealed** | PVC conduit hidden in walls/slabs | Most aesthetic, protected, modern | Costly, difficult to alter, requires careful planning |

> [!NOTE]
> **For modern KTU 2024 board exam context, concealed conduit wiring with PVC conduits is the standard recommendation as per IS 732.**

**BIS Standards Applicable:**

1. **IS 732:1989** — Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations
2. **IS 375:1987** — Symbols and Abbreviations for Electrical Drawings
3. **IS 4648:1968** — Guide for Electrical Layout in Residential Buildings
4. **IS 3043:1987** — Code of Practice for Earthing
5. **IS 694:1990** — PVC Insulated Cables
6. **NEC 2011** — National Electrical Code

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to AVOID:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the Earth wire** in SLD — *Loses 1 mark.*
> 2. **Using wrong MCB rating** (e.g., using 6A for AC circuit) — *Loses 1 mark.*
> 3. **Not specifying the type of wiring** (Concealed/Surface) — *Loses 0.5 mark.*
> 4. **Drawing a non-standard symbol** for sockets, fans, or switches — *Loses 1 mark.*
> 5. **Forgetting the load schedule table** on the drawing sheet — *Loses 1 mark.*
> 6. **Not labelling circuit numbers** on the layout plan — *Loses 0.5 mark.*
> 7. **Confusing 5A and 15A socket symbols** — *Loses 0.5 mark.*
> 8. **Neglecting to mention the height of switch boards** (1.25 m) in the notes — *Loses 0.5 mark.*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this checklist before every revision:**

- [ ] **Standard Supply:** 230V, 1-Phase, 50Hz AC (Domestic); 415V, 3-Phase, 50Hz (Industrial)
- [ ] **Max Load per Lighting Sub-circuit:** 800 W; **Max Points:** 10
- [ ] **Max Load per Power Sub-circuit:** 3000 W
- [ ] **Switch Board Height:** 1.25 m above floor
- [ ] **Socket Height:** 0.3 m (general); 1.2 m (kitchen)
- [ ] **Ceiling Fan Height:** 2.75 m to 3.0 m
- [ ] **Phase Color (BIS):** Red; **Neutral:** Black; **Earth:** Green
- [ ] **Earthing Depth (Pipe):** 2.75 m minimum; **Resistance:** < 5 Ω
- [ ] **Diversity Factor (Residential):** 0.5 to 0.7; **Commercial:** 0.7 to 0.8
- [ ] **Power Factor (Domestic, assumed):** 0.8 lagging
- [ ] **MCB Safety Factor:** 1.25 × calculated current
- [ ] **Standard MCB Ratings:** 6A, 10A, 16A, 20A, 25A, 32A, 40A, 63A
- [ ] **Conduit Fill:** ≤ 40% by cross-sectional area
- [ ] **Voltage Drop Limit:** ≤ 3% of supply voltage
- [ ] **BIS Standards:** IS 732, IS 375, IS 4648, IS 3043, IS 694
- [ ] **Concealed Conduit:** Modern standard for residential buildings
- [ ] **Main Switch:** 32A DP MCB for typical 6 kW residential load
- [ ] **Drawing Components to Always Include:** Title block, scale, legend of symbols, north arrow, load schedule, notes/specifications
- [ ] **Key Formulas:**
  - $I = \dfrac{P}{V \cos\phi}$
  - $\text{Max Demand} = \text{Connected Load} \times \text{DF}$
  - $\text{MCB Rating} = 1.25 \times I_{\text{load}}$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to BIM Software: Draw plan, section and elevation of a single storied residential building (Expt 1 or 2)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL507 - CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to BIM Software: Draw plan, section and elevation of a single storied residential building (Expt 1 or 2)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Building Information Modeling (BIM)** is an intelligent, **3D model-based process** that gives architecture, engineering, and construction (AEC) professionals the insight and tools to more efficiently plan, design, construct, and manage buildings and infrastructure. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme for PCCVL507, BIM is the digital representation of physical and functional characteristics of a facility, forming a shared knowledge resource for information about it.

A **single-storied residential building**, in BIM terminology, is a structure comprising:
- **One floor** (ground floor) above the plinth level
- Wall thickness typically ranging from **200 mm to 300 mm** for external walls
- A pitched/slab roof structure
- Standard room dimensions as per **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment requires students to draw **Plan, Section, and Elevation** of a single-storied residential building using BIM software such as **Revit, ArchiCAD, SketchUp, or AutoCAD with BIM tools**. The drawing must adhere to standard architectural conventions and BIS codes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a house using **Lego blocks** where each block knows what it is (a wall, a door, a window) and remembers information about itself (its material, size, cost, fire rating). Now, instead of physical Lego blocks, you are using **virtual, intelligent digital blocks** on a computer. That is essentially what BIM is.

**Real-World Analogy:**
Think of BIM as a **Google Map of a building**:
- Just as Google Maps shows multiple views (satellite, street, terrain) of the same location,
- BIM shows multiple views (**Plan, Section, Elevation, 3D**) of the same building.
- Change the wall thickness in any one view, and it automatically updates everywhere — like how updating a pin on Google Maps reflects in all views.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from CAD:**
> - **CAD (Computer-Aided Design):** Drawing-based. Each line is independent. If you change a dimension, you must manually update all related views.
> - **BIM (Building Information Modeling):** Object/parameter-based. Each element (wall, door, window) is an intelligent object. Change once, update everywhere.

## 1.3 Three Essential Views of a Building

| View Type | What It Shows | Cut Plane Position |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Top view showing room layout, walls, doors, windows from above | Horizontal cut at **~1.2 m** above floor level |
| **Section** | Vertical cut showing internal construction, floor-to-floor height, foundation depth | Vertical cut through the building |
| **Elevation** | External vertical view showing façade, roof line, openings | No cut — external view |

## 1.4 Standard Dimensions for Single-Storied Residential Building (NBC 2016)

| Component | Standard Dimension |
|---|---|
| **Plinth height** | **450 mm to 600 mm** above ground level |
| **Floor-to-floor height** | **3.0 m to 3.3 m** |
| **Wall thickness (external)** | **200 mm to 300 mm** |
| **Wall thickness (internal)** | **100 mm to 150 mm** |
| **Door size (main)** | **1.0 m × 2.1 m** |
| **Window size** | **1.2 m × 1.2 m** |
| **Verandah/Porch** | **2.0 m to 3.0 m** wide |
| **Plot setbacks** | As per local building rules (**1.0 m to 3.0 m**) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 BIM Workflow Logic — The "Why" and "How"

The BIM process for drawing a single-storied residential building follows a structured, layered approach. Each step builds upon the previous one, ensuring consistency and intelligent data flow.

### Step-by-Step Logic of BIM Drawing Process

**Step 1: Project Setup**
- *Why:* Establishes the coordinate system, units (mm/m), and project parameters.
- *How:* Define project name, location, building type, and levels (Ground Floor, Roof) in the BIM software.

**Step 2: Create Grid Lines**
- *Why:* Grid lines act as a **reference skeleton** to align walls, columns, and structural elements precisely.
- *How:* Draw orthogonal grid lines (typically 3–5 in X-direction and 3–5 in Y-direction) at spacing as per room dimensions.

**Step 3: Place Walls**
- *Why:* Walls are the primary enclosing elements and carry the building's structural and thermal properties.
- *How:* Use the "Wall" tool to draw walls along grid intersections. Assign material (Brick, Concrete) and thickness.

**Step 4: Insert Doors and Windows**
- *Why:* Openings for access, light, and ventilation — must be hosted on walls.
- *How:* Use "Door" and "Window" tools. Click on wall to place. Define size, frame material, and sill height (typically **900 mm** for windows).

**Step 5: Create Floors and Roof**
- *Why:* Define horizontal surfaces and the top enclosure.
