For each required contour elevation $R_C$, a horizontal line is drawn from $R_C$ on the elevation axis; where this horizontal meets the inclined line AB, a perpendicular is dropped to the base line. The base-line foot of the perpendicular gives the position of the contour point along the segment AB at the chosen plan scale. This position is then transferred back to the plan by measurement. **Advantages over arithmetic method:** - Faster when many contour points must be located along a single line. - Reduces computational errors. - More visual and intuitive, especially for irregular slopes approximated by a polyline. - Useful for cross-checking the arithmetic method's results. **(b) Step 1 — Horizontal distance M to N:** $$ D = \sqrt{(260 - 200)^2 + (240 - 200)^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 1600} = \sqrt{5200} = 72.1110 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Contour RLs between 98.40 m and 100.60 m at 1.0 m CI:** Contour RLs: **99.00 m, 100.00 m**. **Step 3 — Apply interpolation:** For $R_C = 99.00$ m: $$ d_{M-C} = 72.1110 \times \frac{99.00 - 98.40}{100.60 - 98.40} = 72.1110 \times \frac{0.60}{2.20} = 19.6666 \text{ m} $$ For $R_C = 100.00$ m: $$ d_{M-C} = 72.1110 \times \frac{100.00 - 98.40}{100.60 - 98.40} = 72.1110 \times \frac{1.60}{2.20} = 52.4444 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4 — Coordinates using section formula:** For 99.00 m contour, ratio = $19.6666 : 52.4444$: $$ X_{99} = \frac{52.4444 \times 200.00 + 19.6666 \times 260.00}{72.1110} = \frac{10488.88 + 5113.32}{72.1110} = 216.34 \text{ m} $$ $$ Y_{99} = \frac{52.4444 \times 200.00 + 19.6666 \times 240.00}{72.1110} = \frac{10488.88 + 4719.98}{72.1110} = 210.67 \text{ m} $$ For 100.00 m contour, ratio = $52.4444 : 19.6666$: $$ X_{100} = \frac{19.6666 \times 200.00 + 52.4444 \times 260.00}{72.1110} = \frac{3933.32 + 13635.54}{72.1110} = 243.66 \text{ m} $$ $$ Y_{100} = \frac{19.6666 \times 200.00 + 52.4444 \times 240.00}{72.1110} = \frac{3933.32 + 12586.66}{72.1110} = 229.33 \text{ m} $$ **Summary Table of Results:** | Contour RL (m) | Distance from M (m) | Easting X (m) | Northing Y (m) | |----------------|----------------------|----------------|------------------| | 99.00 | 19.67 | 216.34 | 210.67 | | 100.00 | 52.44 | 243.66 | 229.33 | These contour points are marked on the plan and joined with similar points from adjacent triangulation lines to produce the final contour map of the site. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module Experiment 2** - **Contour** = line of equal elevation. **Contour Map** = plan view of contours. - **Total Station Output** = (X, Y, Z) = (Easting, Northing, Reduced Level). - **Horizontal distance** between two points: $D = \sqrt{(\Delta X)^2 + (\Delta Y)^2}$. **Never** skip this step. - **Arithmetic interpolation** formula: $d_{AC} = D \times (R_C - R_A) / (R_B - R_A)$. - **Choose CI** based on map purpose, scale, and terrain. Common KTU value: **0.5 m to 1.0 m** for site work. - **Contour lines must be smooth curves**, not straight zig-zag lines. - **Index contour** = every 5th contour, drawn slightly thicker and labelled with its elevation value. - **Contours never cross** each other; they are **perpendicular** to the direction of steepest slope. - **Closed contours with concentric decreasing RL** inward = **depression**; with increasing RL = **hill**. - **Closer spacing** of contours ⇒ **steeper slope**; wider spacing ⇒ **gentler slope**. - **Datum (BM) check** must be done before plotting. - **Interpolation assumption:** uniform slope between any two consecutive surveyed points. - For manual labs, always show the **triangulation network**; for software labs, mention the **interpolation algorithm** used (TIN, IDW, Kriging). - Final map must include: **title block, scale, north arrow, contour-interval note, legend, indexer**.

topics.title: Earthwork estimation from the provided total station survey data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL507 - CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Earthwork estimation from the provided total station survey data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Earthwork Estimation** is the quantitative process of determining the volume of soil (or rock) that must be **excavated (cut)** or **deposited (fill)** to transform the existing natural ground surface into a proposed formation level, computed using the three-dimensional coordinate data (X, Y, Z) obtained from a total station instrument.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme Surveying Lab, the total station provides a dense cloud of **(X, Y, Z) coordinates** along with point identifiers. These coordinates, when interpolated onto a regular grid, yield the **Natural Ground Level (NGL)** at each grid node. The difference between the NGL and the **Proposed Formation Level (PFL)** gives the depth of cut or height of fill at that node. Summing these depths over the area yields the **net earthwork volume in cubic metres (m³)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCCVL507 – Module 2):**
> Students are expected to process raw total station dump data, generate a Digital Elevation Model (DEM) of the site, superimpose the proposed formation plane, and compute the cut/fill volume using at least one classical method (Grid, Cross-Section, or Contour method).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **rectangular sandbox** with a wildly uneven top surface (lumpy, with hillocks and dips). Your task is to flatten the top with a straight-edge screed board to a perfectly level height.

To know **how much sand to remove** from the high spots and **how much to add** to the low spots, you would:

1. **Measure** the height of the sand at many points (a grid of dots).
2. **Decide** a target flat height (formation level).
3. **Calculate** the vertical difference (depth of cut or fill) at every dot.
4. **Multiply** that difference by the area surrounding each dot to get a volume.

Earthwork estimation from total station data is **exactly this process**, performed in a mathematically rigorous manner over hundreds or thousands of grid points spanning an actual construction site. The total station is simply a fast, precise "ruler" for measuring the lumps and dips of the earth.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Grid spacing (h):** Typically **5 m, 10 m, or 20 m**, depending on site uniformity and accuracy required.
- **Volume unit:** **Cubic metres (m³)** for all KTU calculations.
- **Coordinate reference:** Northing (Y), Easting (X), and Elevation (Z) in **metres**, referenced to a Mean Sea Level (MSL) benchmark.
- **Sign convention:** **Cut = Positive (+)**; **Fill = Negative (–)** when reporting net quantities.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline – From Total Station Data to Earthwork Volume

The logical chain is as follows:

1. **Data Acquisition:** The total station logs (X, Y, Z) for every reflected point, usually along a grid, along cross-section lines, or as a random scatter (TIN).
2. **Data Cleaning:** Erroneous points (outliers, zero readings) are removed.
3. **Grid Generation:** A square grid of spacing *h* is overlaid on the survey plan.
4. **NGL Interpolation:** For each grid node, the NGL is interpolated from the surrounding TS points (using bilinear, inverse-distance weighting, or triangulation).
5. **Proposed Level Assignment:** A single formation level (or a designed 3-D surface) is fixed.
6. **Depth Computation:** $d_i = \text{NGL}_i - \text{PFL}_i$ at every grid node *i*.
7. **Volume Computation:** Apply the **Grid Method**, **Cross-Section Method**, or **Contour Method**.
8. **Cut/Fill Separation:** Positive *d* values are summed for cut; negative *d* values (in absolute value) for fill.
9. **Mass-Haul Diagram (optional):** Plots cumulative volume against chainage to plan economical earth movement.

## 2.2 The Three Classical Methods – "Why" and "How"

### Method A – The Grid Method (Most Common with TS Data)
- **Why:** TS data is most conveniently reduced to a square grid; calculation is rapid and uniform.
- **How:** Each grid node is classified by its position (Corner, Edge, Interior) and weighted accordingly.

### Method B – The Cross-Section Method
- **Why:** Used along linear projects (roads, canals, railways) where the centreline is well defined.
- **How:** Cross-sections are taken perpendicular to the centreline at regular intervals *D*. Each section's area *A* is computed, and volume is found by **Trapezoidal** or **Prismoidal** rule.

### Method C – The Contour Method
- **Why:** Useful when only a contour plan (no grid) is available, e.g., from a topo map.
- **How:** Areas enclosed by successive contours are measured with a planimeter, and volume is computed by the average-end-area formula.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use $\vert$ for modulus in math expressions, **never** the bare pipe character inside markdown tables.

| **#** | **Method** | **Governing Formula** | **Variables / Units** |
|:-----:|:-----------|:----------------------|:----------------------|
| 1 | Depth of cut / fill at a node | $d_i \;=\; \text{NGL}_i \;-\; \text{PFL}_i$ | $d_i$ in **m** |
| 2 | Grid Method (weighted mean) | $V \;=\; \dfrac{h^{2}}{4}\,\Big[\,\Sigma\,d_{C} \;+\; 2\,\Sigma\,d_{E} \;+\; 4\,\Sigma\,d_{I}\,\Big]$ | $h$ = grid spacing (**m**), $d_C$ = corner depths, $d_E$ = edge depths, $d_I$ = interior depths (**m**); $V$ in **m³** |
| 3 | Cross-Section: Trapezoidal Rule | $V \;=\; D \cdot \left[\,\dfrac{A_{1}+A_{n}}{2} \;+\; \sum_{i=2}^{n-1} A_{i}\,\right]$ | $D$ = interval between sections (**m**); $A_i$ = cross-sectional area (**m²**); $V$ in **m³** |
| 4 | Cross-Section: Prismoidal Rule | $V \;=\; \dfrac{D}{3}\,\Big[\,A_{1}+A_{n} \;+\; 4\,\Sigma\,A_{\text{odd}} \;+\; 2\,\Sigma\,A_{\text{even}}\,\Big]$ | Requires an **odd** number of sections; $V$ in **m³** |
| 5 | Contour Method (Average End Area) | $V \;=\; h_{c}\cdot\left[\,\dfrac{A_{1}+A_{n}}{2} \;+\; \sum_{i=2}^{n-1} A_{i}\,\right]$ | $h_c$ = contour interval (**m**); $A_i$ = plan area of *i*-th contour (**m²**); $V$ in **m³** |
| 6 | Cut Volume (separation) | $V_{\text{cut}} \;=\; \dfrac{h^{2}}{4}\,\sum\,(d_i \;\text{where}\; d_i>0)\cdot w_i$ | $w_i$ = position weight (1, 2, or 4) |
| 7 | Fill Volume (separation) | $V_{\text{fill}} \;=\; \dfrac{h^{2}}{4}\,\sum\,(\vert d_i \vert \;\text{where}\; d_i<0)\cdot w_i$ | $d_i$ in **m**; $V$ in **m³** |
| 8 | Shrinkage / Swell factor (optional) | $V_{\text{loose}} \;=\; V_{\text{bank}} \cdot (1 + s)$ | $s$ = swell factor (decimal) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highway & Railway Projects:** Earthwork often constitutes **20–30 %** of total project cost; accurate estimation is critical for tendering.
- **Reservoir & Canal Works:** Capacity of a reservoir, or the cut volume of a canal, depends directly on contour-area integration.
- **Site Grading for Buildings & Airports:** Determines how much soil to import or export, impacting logistics and carbon footprint.
- **Landfill Design:** Quantifies cut for cells and fill for capping layers.
- **Mining & Quarrying:** Stockpile volume estimation for monthly production reconciliation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Grid Method Formula

Consider a single square grid cell of side *h*, with corner depths $d_1$, $d_2$, $d_3$, $d_4$ (measured from the proposed level downward, positive for cut).

The volume of cut in that cell is the volume of a **four-sided truncated pyramid (frustum-like prism)** whose upper surface is the natural ground and lower surface is the formation plane.

Averaging the four corner depths (the simplest, equal-weight approach) gives:

$$V_{\text{cell}} \;=\; h^{2} \cdot \frac{d_{1}+d_{2}+d_{3}+d_{4}}{4}$$

Now consider a large grid of $m \times n$ nodes. Each **interior** node is shared by **4 cells**, so it must be counted 4 times in the summation. Each **edge** node is shared by **2 cells**, so it must be counted 2 times. Each **corner** node is shared by **1 cell**, so it must be counted once.

Summing the cell volumes over the whole grid and grouping by node category:

$$V \;=\; \frac{h^{2}}{4} \cdot \Big[\,1\cdot\Sigma d_{C} \;+\; 2\cdot\Sigma d_{E} \;+\; 4\cdot\Sigma d_{I}\,\Big]$$

Hence the **Grid Method Master Formula**:

$$\boxed{\,V \;=\; \dfrac{h^{2}}{4}\,\Big[\,\Sigma\,d_{C} \;+\; 2\,\Sigma\,d_{E} \;+\; 4\,\Sigma\,d_{I}\,\Big]\,}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example – Grid Method (Full Numerical Walk-Through)

**Problem Statement:**
A total station survey of a proposed building site yields the following depths of cut (in metres) at the nodes of a regular **5 × 5 grid** (so 25 nodes in total) with a **grid spacing of h = 10 m**. Compute the total volume of earthwork by the Grid Method.

**Depth Matrix (rows = grid lines 1 → 5, columns = grid lines A → E):**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{1.0} & 1.2 & 1.5 & 1.3 & \textbf{0.8} \\
\hline
1.5 & 1.8 & 2.0 & 1.6 & 1.0 \\
\hline
2.0 & 2.5 & 2.8 & 2.2 & 1.5 \\
\hline
1.8 & 2.2 & 2.5 & 1.9 & 1.2 \\
\hline
\textbf{1.2} & 1.5 & 1.7 & 1.4 & \textbf{0.9} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

(Corner nodes are shown in **bold**.)

### Step 1 – Classify the Nodes

- **Total nodes:** $5 \times 5 = 25$
- **Corner nodes (C):** 4 → positions (1,A), (1,E), (5,A), (5,E)
- **Edge nodes (E):** All perimeter nodes excluding the 4 corners → $5 \times 4 - 4 = 12$
- **Interior nodes (I):** $25 - 4 - 12 = 9$

### Step 2 – Sum the Depths in Each Category

**Sum of Corner Depths (Σ d_C):**
$$d_C = 1.0 + 0.8 + 1.2 + 0.9 = 3.9 \text{ m}$$

**Sum of Edge Depths (Σ d_E) – grouped by side:**
$$\text{Top row (1, B–D):} \; 1.2 + 1.5 + 1.3 = 4.0 \text{ m}$$
$$\text{Bottom row (5, B–D):} \; 1.5 + 1.7 + 1.4 = 4.6 \text{ m}$$
$$\text{Left col (A, 2–4):} \; 1.5 + 2.0 + 1.8 = 5.3 \text{ m}$$
$$\text{Right col (E, 2–4):} \; 1.0 + 1.5 + 1.2 = 3.7 \text{ m}$$
$$\Sigma\,d_E = 4.0 + 4.6 + 5.3 + 3.7 = 17.6 \text{ m}$$

**Sum of Interior Depths (Σ d_I) – rows 2, 3, 4 and columns B, C, D:**
$$\text{Row 2: } 1.8 + 2.0 + 1.6 = 5.4 \text{ m}$$
$$\text{Row 3: } 2.5 + 2.8 + 2.2 = 7.5 \text{ m}$$
$$\text{Row 4: } 2.2 + 2.5 + 1.9 = 6.6 \text{ m}$$
$$\Sigma\,d_I = 5.4 + 7.5 + 6.6 = 19.5 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 – Apply the Grid Method Master Formula

$$V \;=\; \frac{h^{2}}{4}\,\Big[\,\Sigma\,d_{C} \;+\; 2\,\Sigma\,d_{E} \;+\; 4\,\Sigma\,d_{I}\,\Big]$$

Substituting *h* = 10 m, Σ d_C = 3.9, Σ d_E = 17.6, Σ d_I = 19.5:

$$V \;=\; \frac{(10)^{2}}{4}\,\Big[\,3.9 \;+\; 2(17.6) \;+\; 4(19.5)\,\Big]$$

$$V \;=\; \frac{100}{4}\,\Big[\,3.9 \;+\; 35.2 \;+\; 78.0\,\Big]$$

$$V \;=\; 25 \times 117.1$$

$$\boxed{\,V \;=\; 2927.5 \;\text{m}^{3}\,}$$

### Step 4 – Interpretation and Engineering Use

This is the **net cut volume** (all depths were positive, so the entire site is in cut). The contractor must excavate and dispose of **2 927.5 m³** of earth. In practice this value is also reported in **loose cubic metres** (m³-loose) by multiplying by a swell factor, typically **1.20 to 1.30** for common soil.

## 3.3 Cross-Verification with the Cross-Section Method (Trapezoidal)

For pedagogical verification, suppose the same data is reduced to **5 cross-sections** (one per row of the grid), each of width 40 m (4 intervals × 10 m), and the average depth per row is used as a single representative depth to obtain an equivalent rectangular area.

**Average depth per row:**
$$\bar d_1 = \frac{1.0+1.2+1.5+1.3+0.8}{5} = 1.16 \text{ m}$$
$$\bar d_2 = \frac{1.5+1.8+2.0+1.6+1.0}{5} = 1.58 \text{ m}$$
$$\bar d_3 = \frac{2.0+2.5+2.8+2.2+1.5}{5} = 2.20 \text{ m}$$
$$\bar d_4 = \frac{1.8+2.2+2.5+1.9+1.2}{5} = 1.92 \text{ m}$$
$$\bar d_5 = \frac{1.2+1.5+1.7+1.4+0.9}{5} = 1.34 \text{ m}$$

**Equivalent area per section** (width 40 m):
$$A_i = 40 \times \bar d_i \;\Rightarrow\; 46.4,\; 63.2,\; 88.0,\; 76.8,\; 53.6 \;\text{m}^{2}$$

**Trapezoidal Rule with D = 10 m (row-to-row distance):**
$$V \;=\; D \cdot \left[\frac{A_1+A_5}{2} + A_2 + A_3 + A_4\right]$$
$$V \;=\; 10 \cdot \left[\frac{46.4+53.6}{2} + 63.2 + 88.0 + 76.8\right]$$
$$V \;=\; 10 \cdot \left[50.0 + 228.0\right] = 2780.0 \;\text{m}^{3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two methods yield **2 927.5 m³** (grid) vs **2 780.0 m³** (cross-section average). The difference (~**5.0 %**) arises because the cross-section method here uses **row-averaged depths**, a coarse simplification. The grid method is the **more accurate** procedure and is preferred when dense TS data is available — this is a frequent KTU viva question.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Method Selection

| **Site Characteristic** | **Recommended Method** | **Reason** |
|:------------------------|:-----------------------|:-----------|
| Dense TS point cloud on flat site | Grid Method | Fast, accurate, no planimeter required |
| Linear project (road, canal) | Cross-Section Method | Natural alignment with centreline |
| Hilly site with sparse data | Contour Method | Compatible with topo-map output |
| Complex 3-D proposed surface (e.g., sport field with slopes) | Grid + weighted PFL | Each node has its own target level |
| Need for very high accuracy (airport runway) | Prismoidal on cross-sections | Conical-section correction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Process Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TS Field Survey: Capture X, Y, Z] --> B[Data Transfer to PC via CSV]
    B --> C[Clean Data: Remove Outliers and Zeros]
    C --> D[Overlay Square Grid of Spacing h]
    D --> E[Interpolate NGL at Every Grid Node]
    F[Fix Proposed Formation Level PFL] --> G[Compute Depth d = NGL - PFL]
    E --> G
    G --> H{Choose Volume Method}
    H -->|Grid| I[Classify Nodes: Corner, Edge, Interior]
    I --> J[Apply Weighted Grid Formula]
    H -->|Cross-Section| K[Compute Section Areas A sub i]
    K --> L[Apply Trapezoidal or Prismoidal Rule]
    H -->|Contour| M[Planimeter Areas of Contour Bands]
    M --> N[Apply Average End Area Formula]
    J --> O[Sum Cut and Fill Separately]
    L --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Apply Swell or Shrinkage Factor]
    P --> Q[Final Earthwork Quantity in m cubed]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        A1[TS Raw Coordinates X, Y, Z]
        A2[Benchmark RL]
        A3[Proposed Formation Level]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Processing Layer]
        B1[Coordinate Transformation and Grid Setup]
        B2[NGL Interpolation Module]
        B3[Depth Computation Engine]
        B4[Volume Calculator: Grid, Section, Contour]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        C1[Cut Volume in m cubed]
        C2[Fill Volume in m cubed]
        C3[Net Volume and Mass Haul]
        C4[3D Cut-Fill Contour Map]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B4 --> C3
    B4 --> C4
```

## 4.3 Position-Weighting Schematic (Grid Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Corner Node: weight 1] --> S[Single Cell Contribution]
    P2[Edge Node: weight 2] --> T[Shared by 2 Cells]
    P3[Interior Node: weight 4] --> U[Shared by 4 Cells]
    S --> SUM[Sum: 1 dC plus 2 dE plus 4 dI]
    T --> SUM
    U --> SUM
    SUM --> FORMULA[V = h squared over 4 times Sum]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Sketch of Cut / Fill Geometry

```
           Proposed Formation Plane (PFL)  ───────────────────  (target)
                                             │       │
            NGL (Natural Ground)             │ d_cut │ d_fill
            (hilly profile)                   │       │
              ╱╲       ╱╲   ╱╲                │       │
   ─────────╱──╲─────╱──╲─╱──╲────────────────────── 
            ↑           ↑      ↑
         Corner     Edge    Interior
         node       node     node
       (weight 1) (weight 2) (weight 4)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Define earthwork estimation. List the three classical methods used for computing earthwork volume from total station data.** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
Earthwork estimation is the process of calculating the volume of soil to be excavated (cut) or deposited (fill) in order to convert the existing natural ground surface into a proposed formation level, using the 3-D coordinate data captured by a total station.
The three classical methods are:
1. **Grid Method**
2. **Cross-Section Method** (with Trapezoidal or Prismoidal rule)
3. **Contour Method**

*(1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for naming all three, 1 mark for distinguishing them)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**State the prismoidal formula for earthwork volume. Under what condition is it applicable?** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
For cross-sectional areas $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ at equal intervals $D$:
$$V \;=\; \frac{D}{3}\,\Big[\,A_{1}+A_{n} \;+\; 4\,\Sigma\,A_{\text{odd}} \;+\; 2\,\Sigma\,A_{\text{even}}\,\Big]$$
**Condition:** The number of sections *n* must be **odd** (i.e., an even number of intervals), because Simpson's 1/3 rule requires a parabolic fit between pairs of sections.

*(1.5 marks for formula, 1.5 marks for condition)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]` — Module 2 Internal Choice Option I

#### (a) With a neat sketch, explain the **Grid Method** of earthwork estimation. How are corner, edge, and interior grid points treated in the volume formula? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A square grid of spacing *h* is overlaid on the survey plan, and the depth of cut / fill is determined at each node. The nodes fall into three classes:

| **Node Class** | **Count Contribution** | **Weight in Formula** | **Reason** |
|:---------------|:----------------------:|:---------------------:|:-----------|
| Corner | Shared by 1 cell | **1** | Lies on a single grid square |
| Edge | Shared by 2 cells | **2** | Lies on the boundary of 2 cells |
| Interior | Shared by 4 cells | **4** | Lies at the junction of 4 cells |

The **Master Formula** is:

$$V \;=\; \frac{h^{2}}{4}\Big[\,\Sigma\,d_{C} + 2\,\Sigma\,d_{E} + 4\,\Sigma\,d_{I}\,\Big]$$

The factor $h^{2}/4$ arises because the area of one cell is $h^{2}$ and each corner depth contributes only **1/4** of a cell's full volume weight.

*(3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for classification logic, 2 marks for formula and explanation)*

#### (b) The following **depths of cut (in m)** were observed at the corners of a **4 × 4 square grid of 5 m spacing**. Calculate the total volume of cut by the Grid Method. (7 marks)

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
0.8 & 1.2 & 1.0 & 0.6 \\
\hline
1.5 & 2.0 & 1.8 & 1.1 \\
\hline
1.7 & 2.4 & 2.1 & 1.4 \\
\hline
0.9 & 1.3 & 1.2 & 0.7 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Identify the 16 nodes:**
- Corners (C) = 4 → 0.8, 0.6, 0.9, 0.7
- Edges (E) = 8 → top: 1.2, 1.0; bottom: 1.3, 1.2; left: 1.5, 1.7; right: 1.1, 1.4
- Interior (I) = 4 → 2.0, 1.8, 2.4, 2.1

**Step 2 – Sum each category:**
$$\Sigma\,d_C = 0.8 + 0.6 + 0.9 + 0.7 = 3.0 \text{ m}$$
$$\Sigma\,d_E = 1.2+1.0+1.3+1.2+1.5+1.7+1.1+1.4 = 10.4 \text{ m}$$
$$\Sigma\,d_I = 2.0+1.8+2.4+2.1 = 8.3 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 – Apply the formula** with *h* = 5 m:
$$V = \frac{5^{2}}{4}\,\Big[\,3.0 + 2(10.4) + 4(8.3)\,\Big]$$
$$V = \frac{25}{4}\,\Big[\,3.0 + 20.8 + 33.2\,\Big]$$
$$V = 6.25 \times 57.0$$
$$\boxed{\,V = 356.25 \;\text{m}^{3}\,}$$

*(1 mark for classification, 2 marks for sums, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final answer with units)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` — Module 2 Internal Choice Option II

#### (a) Explain the **Cross-Section Method** and **Contour Method** of volume calculation, with neat sketches. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Cross-Section Method:**
Sections are taken perpendicular to the project centreline at regular interval *D*. Each section is a transverse profile of the ground; its end area $A_i$ is computed by the trapezoidal rule on the offsets and depths:
$$A_i = D_{\text{offset}} \cdot \left[\frac{d_1+d_n}{2} + d_2 + d_3 + \cdots + d_{n-1}\right]$$
Volume between two consecutive sections is then computed by:
- **Trapezoidal Rule:** $V = D \cdot \frac{A_i + A_{i+1}}{2}$
- **Prismoidal Rule:** $V = \frac{D}{6}(A_i + 4A_m + A_{i+1})$, where $A_m$ is the mid-section area.

**Contour Method:**
A contour plan is divided into horizontal bands between successive contours of interval $h_c$. The plan area $A_i$ of each contour is measured with a planimeter. Volume is:
$$V = h_c\left[\frac{A_1+A_n}{2} + \sum_{i=2}^{n-1} A_i\right]$$
This is essentially the average-end-area formula applied in the vertical direction.

*(3 marks for cross-section explanation, 3 marks for contour method, 1 mark for diagrams)*

#### (b) The cross-sectional areas at **five successive sections 10 m apart** along a proposed road are: $A_1 = 25 \;\text{m}^{2}$, $A_2 = 38 \;\text{m}^{2}$, $A_3 = 45 \;\text{m}^{2}$, $A_4 = 52 \;\text{m}^{2}$, $A_5 = 40 \;\text{m}^{2}$. Calculate the volume of earthwork by (i) the Trapezoidal rule, and (ii) the Prismoidal rule. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Trapezoidal Rule:**
$$V = D\left[\frac{A_1 + A_5}{2} + A_2 + A_3 + A_4\right]$$
$$V = 10\left[\frac{25+40}{2} + 38 + 45 + 52\right]$$
$$V = 10\left[32.5 + 135\right] = 10 \times 167.5$$
$$\boxed{\,V_{\text{trap}} = 1675 \;\text{m}^{3}\,}$$

**(ii) Prismoidal Rule:** *n* = 5 (odd) ✔ applicable
$$V = \frac{D}{3}\Big[A_1 + A_5 + 4(A_2 + A_4) + 2(A_3)\Big]$$
$$V = \frac{10}{3}\Big[25 + 40 + 4(38+52) + 2(45)\Big]$$
$$V = \frac{10}{3}\Big[65 + 360 + 90\Big] = \frac{10 \times 515}{3}$$
$$\boxed{\,V_{\text{prism}} = 1716.67 \;\text{m}^{3}\,}$$

*(1 mark for trapezoidal formula, 2 marks for answer; 1 mark for prismoidal formula, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final answer)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $h^2/4$ factor** in the grid method — many students use just $h^2$. This yields a 4× error.
> 2. **Applying the prismoidal rule with an even number of sections** — Simpson's 1/3 rule demands an odd *n*; otherwise the formula is invalid.
> 3. **Mixing up units** — grid spacing *h* in metres, area in m², volume in m³. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 4. **Summing cut and fill as a single net value** without reporting them separately. Always state $V_{\text{cut}}$ and $V_{\text{fill}}$ distinctly, even when one of them is zero.
> 5. **Omitting the sign convention** when depths are negative (fill). Take the absolute value before weighting.
> 6. **Using row-averaged cross-section areas** as if they were mid-section values in the prismoidal rule — *A_m* must be an actual intermediate section, not an average.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (save this for the night before the exam):**

- **Definition:** Earthwork estimation = volume of cut + volume of fill between NGL and PFL.
- **Inputs from TS:** X, Y, Z coordinates; reduced to RLs relative to a benchmark.
- **Three methods:** Grid (most common), Cross-Section, Contour.
- **Grid Method weights:** Corner = **1**, Edge = **2**, Interior = **4**.
- **Grid Method formula:** $V = \dfrac{h^{2}}{4}\big[\Sigma d_C + 2\Sigma d_E + 4\Sigma d_I\big]$.
- **Trapezoidal rule for sections:** $V = D\Big[\frac{A_1+A_n}{2} + \sum A_{\text{intermediate}}\Big]$.
- **Prismoidal rule:** $V = \dfrac{D}{3}\big[A_1+A_n + 4\Sigma A_{\text{odd}} + 2\Sigma A_{\text{even}}\big]$; requires **odd** number of sections.
- **Contour method:** $V = h_c\Big[\frac{A_1+A_n}{2} + \sum A_{\text{intermediate}}\Big]$, with $h_c$ = contour interval.
- **Sign convention:** Cut = positive depth; Fill = negative depth (take modulus for volume).
- **Always report both** $V_{\text{cut}}$ and $V_{\text{fill}}$ in **m³**, even if one is zero.
- **Net volume** = $V_{\text{cut}} - V_{\text{fill}}$; borrow or export the balance.
- **Swell factor** (typical): sandy soil ≈ 1.10–1.15; clayey soil ≈ 1.20–1.30; rock ≈ 1.30–1.50. Apply when reporting as *loose* volume.
- **KTU favourite trick question:** "Why is the prismoidal rule considered more accurate than the trapezoidal rule?" — Answer: it assumes the volume between two sections varies as a **second-degree (parabolic) function** rather than linearly, providing a true average end-area correction via the mid-section.
- **Common viva line:** "Why is $h^2/4$ the prefactor?" — Because a corner node belongs to only **1/4** of its surrounding cell, and we sum corner contributions with weight 1; hence the cell-area factor $h^2$ must be divided by 4.
- **Tool tip:** In the KTU Modelling Lab, the entire process is usually implemented in a spreadsheet (Excel/Google Sheets) using absolute references and the SUMPRODUCT function for rapid grid-method evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of a small water supply pipe network using EPANET
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL507 - CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of a small water supply pipe network using EPANET

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EPANET** is a public-domain, hydraulic modelling software developed by the **U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA)** that performs **extended-period simulation (EPS)** of hydraulic and water-quality behaviour within pressurized pipe networks. In the KTU 2024 Scheme PCCVL507 syllabus, it is used as the standard numerical laboratory tool to model **small water distribution networks (WDS)**, computing nodal pressures, pipe flow rates, head losses, tank water levels, and chlorine/residual concentrations over a defined simulation duration.

A **Water Supply Pipe Network** (also called a Water Distribution System, WDS) is an interconnected graph of **pipes**, **nodes (junctions)**, **pumps**, **valves**, **storage tanks**, and **reservoirs** that delivers treated water from the source to the consumer at adequate **pressure (m H₂O)**, **flow (L/s)**, and **quality (residual chlorine)**.

**Key Terminology Matrix (KTU Expected Vocabulary):**

| Term | Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Node / Junction | J | — | Pipe intersection where demand is withdrawn |
| Pipe / Link | P | — | Conduit conveying water between two nodes |
| Reservoir | R | — | Infinite source/sink (e.g., treatment plant clear well) |
| Tank | T | m³ | Variable-head storage element |
| Elevation | z | m | Vertical datum of a node above mean sea level |
| Demand | D | L/s | Water withdrawn at a node |
| Head | H | m | Energy per unit weight of water |
| Pressure | p | m H₂O | Head minus elevation |
| Flow | Q | L/s or m³/s | Discharge through a pipe |
| Diameter | D | mm | Internal pipe diameter |
| Length | L | m | Pipe length between two nodes |
| Hazen-Williams C | C | — | Roughness coefficient (unitless) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of EPANET like the "Circuit Solver for Water".**
> Just as Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) lets electrical engineers solve for unknown currents and voltages in a circuit, EPANET applies the **Continuity Equation** (sum of inflows = sum of outflows at every node) and the **Energy Equation** (head loss between connected nodes) to solve for unknown **flows** and **pressures** in a pipe network.

**A real-world analogy:**

Imagine a **building's plumbing system** with multiple floors, branch pipes, and storage tanks. If you open all taps simultaneously, the lower floors get strong flow while the top floor may dribble. EPANET predicts this **before the building is constructed** by mathematically simulating the network. The same logic extends to a city — but with hundreds of pipes, multiple pumps, elevated service reservoirs (ESR), and varying demand patterns through 24 hours.

**Why it matters in Civil Engineering:**

1. **Design Verification** — ensures every consumer receives $\geq 7$ m pressure (for single-storey) to $\geq 17$ m (for multi-storey) as per **CPHEEO Manual**.
2. **Pump Selection** — identifies the required Total Dynamic Head (TDH).
3. **Residual Chlorine Tracking** — checks water-quality decay throughout the network.
4. **Cost Optimization** — avoids over-designing pipe diameters and pumping energy.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard benchmarks (CPHEEO, India):**
> - Minimum residual pressure at consumer tap: **7.0 m H₂O** (single-storey); **12.0–17.0 m H₂O** (multi-storey).
> - Minimum residual chlorine at the network extremity: **0.2 mg/L** (free chlorine).
> - Standard design velocity range: **0.6 m/s to 2.4 m/s**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Governing Physics Behind EPANET

EPANET internally solves the following three classical equations at every time step:

### 2.1.1 Node-Flow Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

For every junction $J_i$ in the network, the algebraic sum of all inflows and outflows must equal the nodal demand:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} Q_{ik} - D_i = 0$$

where:
- $Q_{ik}$ = flow in pipe connecting junction $i$ to junction $k$ (m³/s)
- $D_i$ = demand at junction $i$ (m³/s)
- $n$ = number of pipes meeting at junction $i$

**The "Why":** Water is incompressible and the network is a closed-loop graph. Whatever flows in must flow out (Kirchhoff's current-law analogue).

---

### 2.1.2 Pipe Head-Loss Equation (Conservation of Energy)

For each pipe between junctions $i$ and $k$, the energy loss equals the difference in total head:

$$H_i - H_k = h_f$$

where $H_i$ = total head at node $i$ and $H_k$ = total head at node $k$, and $h_f$ is the frictional head loss.

**EPANET offers three head-loss models** (the user selects one globally):

**Model 1 — Hazen-Williams (most common for water networks):**

$$h_f = \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$$

where:
- $L$ = pipe length (m)
- $Q$ = flow rate (m³/s)
- $D$ = internal diameter (m)
- $C$ = Hazen-Williams roughness coefficient (unitless)
- $h_f$ = head loss (m)

**Model 2 — Darcy-Weisbach (most rigorous, valid for all fluids):**

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

where:
- $f$ = Darcy friction factor (from Moody chart / Colebrook equation)
- $V$ = mean flow velocity = $Q/A$ (m/s)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²
- $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²)

**Model 3 — Chezy-Manning (used for open channels and rough pipes):**

$$h_f = \frac{10.29 \cdot n^2 \cdot L \cdot Q^2}{D^{5.333}}$$

where $n$ = Manning's roughness coefficient (s/m^(1/3)).

---

### 2.1.3 Storage Tank Continuity (Dynamic Mass Balance)

For a tank with cross-sectional area $A_T$:

$$A_T \cdot \frac{dH_T}{dt} = \sum_{p=1}^{m} Q_p - D_T$$

where:
- $H_T$ = water surface elevation in the tank (m)
- $Q_p$ = flow in/out of each connecting pipe (m³/s)
- $D_T$ = demand directly assigned to the tank (m³/s)
- $m$ = number of pipes connected to the tank

**The "How":** As water enters, the tank level rises; as water exits, it falls. EPANET uses a small time step (default 1 hour, modifiable) to integrate this ODE.

---

## 2.2 Solution Algorithm: Gradient Method (Todini & Pilati, 1987)

EPANET uses the **Global Gradient Algorithm (GGA)** — a Newton-Raphson-based solver that simultaneously solves for nodal heads and pipe flows in a single matrix inversion at each time step:

$$\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{F}$$

where $\mathbf{A}$ is the network admittance-like matrix (size $N \times N$ for $N$ nodes), $\mathbf{H}$ is the unknown head vector, and $\mathbf{F}$ contains demands, fixed heads, and pump contributions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU lab viva:** A common examiner question is *"Which numerical method does EPANET use?"* The correct answer is the **Global Gradient Algorithm (GGA) proposed by Todini and Pilati (1987)**, which supersedes the earlier Hardy-Cross method. GGA converges in 3–5 iterations for most networks.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the table below for the 2-hour lab examination. Every EPANET lab viva question draws from these formulas.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Typical Use / When to Apply |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Continuity at node | $\sum Q_{in} = \sum Q_{out} + D$ | Mass conservation check |
| 2 | Total head | $H = z + \frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | Energy per unit weight |
| 3 | Pressure head | $h_p = H - z$ | Convert head → pressure |
| 4 | Hazen-Williams | $h_f = \frac{10.67 L Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} D^{4.87}}$ | Default water-network model |
| 5 | Darcy-Weisbach | $h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | Rigorous, laminar+turbulent |
| 6 | Chezy-Manning | $h_f = \frac{10.29 n^2 L Q^2}{D^{5.333}}$ | Rough pipes / open channels |
| 7 | Velocity | $V = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Reynolds number, velocity check |
| 8 | Reynolds number | $Re = \frac{VD}{\nu}$ | Flow regime identification |
| 9 | Pumping head | $H_{pump} = A - B \cdot Q^2$ | Single-point pump curve |
| 10 | Power required | $P = \frac{\rho g Q H_p}{\eta}$ | Pump sizing in kW |
| 11 | Tank mass balance | $A_T \frac{dH_T}{dt} = \sum Q_p - D_T$ | Tank level vs. time |
| 12 | First-order chlorine decay | $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$ | Water quality analysis |

> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE (Exam Pitfall):** In the table above, all vertical separators have been written as textual descriptions (e.g., "head loss") to avoid breaking the markdown table. When writing inside an exam answer, use $\vert$ or $\mid$ (LaTeX) — never the keyboard pipe character.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> EPANET is used in industry for:
> 1. **Urban Water Supply Design** — sizing of pipes, ESR, and pumps.
> 2. **Fire-Flow Analysis** — checking residual pressure when a hydrant discharges.
> 3. **Water Age & Quality** — identifying stagnation zones where chlorine decays below 0.2 mg/L.
> 4. **Energy Auditing** — computing daily pumping cost in ₹/kWh.
> 5. **Disaster Scenario Modelling** — pipe burst, pump failure, and contamination event.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hazen-Williams Head-Loss Equation

> [!NOTE]
> The Hazen-Williams equation is an **empirical** formula (not derived from first principles) developed in 1903. EPANET uses it as the default for municipal water networks because of its simplicity and proven accuracy for $V < 3$ m/s and $D \geq 50$ mm. The derivation below is the **dimensional-analysis-based reconstruction** commonly shown in KTU exams.

**Step 1 — List the physical variables that influence head loss in a pipe:**

$$h_f = f(L, D, V, g, \rho, \mu, k_s)$$

where $k_s$ is the wall roughness. There are 7 variables in 3 fundamental dimensions (M, L, T), giving 4 dimensionless $\pi$ groups.

**Step 2 — Form the dimensionless groups (Buckingham $\pi$ Theorem):**

$$\pi_1 = \frac{h_f}{D}, \quad \pi_2 = \frac{L}{D}, \quad \pi_3 = \frac{VD}{\nu} = Re, \quad \pi_4 = \frac{k_s}{D}$$

So $h_f/D$ is a function of the other three:

$$\frac{h_f}{D} = \phi\!\left(\frac{L}{D}, Re, \frac{k_s}{D}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the empirical simplifications of Hazen & Williams:**

(a) For fully turbulent flow in smooth-to-moderately rough commercial pipes, the $Re$ dependence is weak.
(b) The roughness effect is captured by a single coefficient $C$ (not $k_s/D$).

The empirical result is:

$$V = 0.8492 \cdot C \cdot R_h^{0.63} \cdot S^{0.54}$$

where $R_h$ = hydraulic radius (for full pipe, $R_h = D/4$) and $S$ = $h_f/L$.

**Step 4 — Convert velocity form to discharge form:**

$$Q = A \cdot V = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot 0.8492 \cdot C \cdot \left(\frac{D}{4}\right)^{0.63} \cdot \left(\frac{h_f}{L}\right)^{0.54}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $h_f$ explicitly:**

$$Q = 0.2785 \cdot C \cdot D^{2.63} \cdot \left(\frac{h_f}{L}\right)^{0.54}$$

Raising both sides to the power $1/0.54 = 1.852$:

$$Q^{1.852} = 0.2785^{1.852} \cdot C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87} \cdot \frac{h_f}{L}$$

Solving for $h_f$:

$$h_f = \frac{0.2785^{-1.852} \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$$

Computing the constant $0.2785^{-1.852} \approx 10.67$:

$$\boxed{h_f = \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}}$$

This is the **SI Hazen-Williams formula** (when $L$ in m, $D$ in m, $Q$ in m³/s). The **FPS version** uses **4.73** as the constant when units are in feet, gallons/min, and inches.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Continuity Equation at a Node

For a junction with $n$ pipes connected, applying conservation of mass (steady-state, incompressible):

$$Q_1 + Q_2 + \ldots + Q_n - D = 0$$

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} Q_j = D$$

A **sign convention** is mandatory (EPANET convention):
- **Inflow** into the node → **positive**
- **Outflow** from the node → **negative**
- **Demand** at the node → **positive (withdrawal)**

Hence:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} Q_{ij} - D_i = 0 \quad \text{for every node } i$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: 2-Pipe Branched Network (KTU Lab Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked example matching the typical 14-mark KTU lab problem.**

**Network:**

```
   J1 (Reservoir, z = 100 m) -----Pipe 1 (L=500 m, D=200 mm, C=120)----- J2
                                                                       |
                                                              Pipe 2 (L=300 m, D=150 mm, C=100)
                                                                       |
                                                                     J3 (z = 95 m, D=10 L/s)
```

The demand at J3 is 10 L/s. Find the **flow in Pipe 1**, **flow in Pipe 2**, and **pressure at J3**.

**Given:**

- $z_1 = 100$ m, $z_3 = 95$ m
- Pipe 1: $L_1 = 500$ m, $D_1 = 0.200$ m, $C_1 = 120$
- Pipe 2: $L_2 = 300$ m, $D_2 = 0.150$ m, $C_2 = 100$
- $Q_2 = 10$ L/s $= 0.010$ m³/s (the demand at J3, equals flow in Pipe 2)
- $Q_1 = 0.010$ m³/s (by continuity at J2: $Q_{1} = Q_{2}$ since no demand at J2)

**Step 1 — Head loss in Pipe 1:**

$$h_{f1} = \frac{10.67 \cdot L_1 \cdot Q_1^{1.852}}{C_1^{1.852} \cdot D_1^{4.87}}$$

Numerator:
$$10.67 \cdot 500 \cdot (0.010)^{1.852} = 10.67 \cdot 500 \cdot 1.4125 \times 10^{-4} = 0.7536$$

Denominator:
$$120^{1.852} \cdot (0.200)^{4.87} = 6161.4 \cdot 8.913 \times 10^{-5} = 0.5491$$

$$h_{f1} = \frac{0.7536}{0.5491} = 1.372 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Head loss in Pipe 2:**

$$h_{f2} = \frac{10.67 \cdot 300 \cdot (0.010)^{1.852}}{100^{1.852} \cdot (0.150)^{4.87}}$$

Numerator:
$$10.67 \cdot 300 \cdot 1.4125 \times 10^{-4} = 0.4522$$

Denominator:
$$100^{1.852} \cdot (0.150)^{4.87} = 6309.6 \cdot 2.516 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5875$$

$$h_{f2} = \frac{0.4522}{1.5875} = 0.285 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Total head at J3:**

$$H_3 = H_1 - h_{f1} - h_{f2} = 100 - 1.372 - 0.285 = 98.343 \text{ m}$$

(Since the reservoir has $V \approx 0$ and $p_{atm} = 0$ gauge, $H_1 = z_1 = 100$ m.)

**Step 4 — Pressure head at J3:**

$$p_3 = H_3 - z_3 = 98.343 - 95.0 = 3.343 \text{ m H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 5 — Velocity check in Pipe 1:**

$$V_1 = \frac{4Q_1}{\pi D_1^2} = \frac{4 \cdot 0.010}{\pi \cdot 0.04} = 0.318 \text{ m/s}$$

This is **below** the 0.6 m/s minimum (CPHEEO). The pipe would be flagged for **possible stagnation** — a real design consequence of modelling.

**Step 6 — Velocity check in Pipe 2:**

$$V_2 = \frac{4Q_2}{\pi D_2^2} = \frac{4 \cdot 0.010}{\pi \cdot 0.0225} = 0.566 \text{ m/s}$$

Slightly below 0.6 m/s — borderline, design should consider a 125 mm diameter reduction only if hydraulically feasible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result Table (for exam answer sheet):**

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_1$ (Pipe 1) | 0.010 | m³/s (10 L/s) |
| $Q_2$ (Pipe 2) | 0.010 | m³/s (10 L/s) |
| $h_{f1}$ | 1.372 | m |
| $h_{f2}$ | 0.285 | m |
| $H_3$ (Total head) | 98.343 | m |
| $p_3$ (Pressure) | 3.343 | m H₂O |
| $V_1$ | 0.318 | m/s |
| $V_2$ | 0.566 | m/s |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Typical EPANET Lab Output Variables

> [!TIP]
> When running an EPANET simulation, the **Report Generator** produces a Time-Series table. For the lab record, the student must extract the following:

| EPANET Table | Column Headers | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| Node Table | Node ID, Elevation, Base Demand, Head, Pressure, Quality | Verify pressure $\geq 7$ m H₂O |
| Link Table | Link ID, Length, Diameter, Roughness, Flow, Velocity, Headloss, Friction Factor | Check velocity in 0.6–2.4 m/s |
| Tank Table | Tank ID, Elevation, Initial Level, Max Level, Min Level, Volume, Inflow | Confirm overflow/emptiness |
| Energy Table | Pump ID, % Utilization, Avg. Power, Peak Power, Cost/day | Energy audit |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Step-by-Step EPANET Simulation Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram is the **canonical procedure** every KTU student must follow during the lab. Memorize it; it appears in viva questions as "Explain the steps in performing an EPANET simulation."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Network Goal] --> B[Step1: Draw Network Schematic in EPANET Map]
    B --> C[Step2: Add Junctions J1 J2 J3 and Label Elevations]
    C --> D[Step3: Add Reservoir R1 at Source]
    D --> E[Step4: Add Storage Tank T1 if Required]
    E --> F[Step5: Draw Pipes and Set Length Diameter Roughness]
    F --> G[Step6: Add Pump if Required]
    G --> H[Step7: Assign Base Demand at Each Junction L per s]
    H --> I[Step8: Select Head Loss Formula Hazen Williams]
    I --> J[Step9: Set Simulation Duration Example 24 hours]
    J --> K[Step10: Run Analysis]
    K --> L{All Nodes Pressurized Above 7 m}
    L -- No --> M[Iterate: Increase Diameter or Add Pump]
    M --> F
    L -- Yes --> N[Step11: Generate Report and Plot Graphs]
    N --> O[Step12: Verify Velocities between 0.6 and 2.4 m per s]
    O --> P[Step13: Document and Submit Lab Record]
    P --> Q[End]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — EPANET Network Component Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level diagram shows the **functional relationships** between the major EPANET objects. Use it for viva questions like "List the components of a water-distribution model in EPANET."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SourceSide[Source Components]
        R1[Reservoir R1: Infinite Source]
        T1[Tank T1: Variable Head Storage]
    end

    subgraph Conveyance[Conveyance Components]
        Pump1[Pump P1: Adds Energy Head]
        Pipe1[Pipe 1: L D C Specified]
        Pipe2[Pipe 2: L D C Specified]
        Valve1[Valve V1: PRV FCV etc]
    end

    subgraph DemandSide[Demand Components]
        J1[Junction J1: Node with Demand]
        J2[Junction J2: Node with Demand]
        J3[Junction J3: Node with Demand]
    end

    R1 --> Pipe1
    Pipe1 --> Pump1
    Pump1 --> Pipe2
    Pipe2 --> T1
    T1 --> J1
    Pipe1 --> J2
    Pipe2 --> J3
    Pipe2 -.Pressure Regulating.-> Valve1
    Valve1 --> J2
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Data Flow in EPANET Solver (Gradient Method)

> [!NOTE]
> A deeper view of the **internal computational engine** — useful for higher-marks viva questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    In1[Input: Network INP file] --> Parse[Parser: Reads Junctions Pipes Pumps Tanks]
    Parse --> Topo[Topology Builder: Node Link Incidence Matrix]
    Topo --> Init[Initialize Heads and Flows]
    Init --> Loop{Iterate: Solve GGA Linear System}
    Loop -->|Residual greater than 1e-5| Update[Update H and Q using Newton Raphson]
    Update --> Loop
    Loop -->|Converged| Time[Advance to Next Time Step]
    Time --> EndCheck{Simulation Duration Reached}
    EndCheck -- No --> Init
    EndCheck -- Yes --> Report[Generate Node Link Tank Energy Reports]
    Report --> Out[Output: Tables Plots Binary File]
```

---

| Object | EPANET Symbol | Required Inputs | Output Check |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reservoir | R | Total Head (m) | Acts as fixed-H boundary |
| Junction | J | Elevation, Base Demand, Pattern | Pressure (m H₂O) |
| Tank | T | Elevation, Min/Max Level, Diameter | Water Surface Elevation |
| Pipe | P | Length, Diameter, Roughness, C | Flow, Velocity, Headloss |
| Pump | Pump | Power, Curve (H vs Q) | Energy Added, % Utilisation |
| Valve | V | Type, Setting, Diameter | Head Loss / Flow Control |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define the term "Extended Period Simulation (EPS)" in the context of EPANET. How is it different from a "Steady-State Simulation"?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Extended Period Simulation (EPS)** is an analysis in which EPANET computes nodal heads, pipe flows, tank levels, and water-quality concentrations over a sequence of **time steps** (e.g., 1-hour intervals over 24 hours), allowing demand patterns to vary with time. This captures the dynamic behaviour of tanks filling and emptying, pumps switching on/off, and diurnal demand variation.
>
> In contrast, a **Steady-State Simulation** assumes all demands and tank levels are fixed at a single instant — producing a "snapshot" of the network at peak or average demand without any temporal evolution.
>
> *EPS* is preferred for design verification of storages, pump scheduling, and residual chlorine tracking; *steady-state* is used only for quick checks at peak hour. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: List the three head-loss formulas available in EPANET and state one practical situation where each is most appropriate.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | # | Formula | Best Applicable Situation |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | **Hazen-Williams** | Municipal water supply with $D \geq 50$ mm and $V \leq 3$ m/s — the **default** in EPANET. |
> | 2 | **Darcy-Weisbach** | Highly accurate analysis, valid for all Reynolds numbers; used when pipe material varies (RCC, DI, MS, PVC) and for very smooth or very rough pipes. |
> | 3 | **Chezy-Manning** | Large trunk mains and rough concrete pipes; sometimes used in Indian rural water-supply schemes. |
>
> **(3 marks — 1 mark per formula + correct situation.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 2]`

**Q: A two-reservoir network consists of a higher reservoir R₁ (water surface elevation 120 m) connected to a lower reservoir R₂ (water surface elevation 80 m) through a single pipe of length 1500 m and diameter 250 mm. The Hazen-Williams coefficient is 110.**

**(a)** Compute the steady-state flow rate $Q$ in the pipe using the Hazen-Williams equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If a junction J is located 600 m from R₁ along the same pipe and has an elevation of 105 m, find the **pressure head at J** and the **flow velocity**. State whether the design complies with CPHEEO velocity norms. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Given:**

- $\Delta H = H_{R_1} - H_{R_2} = 120 - 80 = 40$ m (available head)
- $L = 1500$ m, $D = 0.250$ m, $C = 110$
- For Hazen-Williams: $h_f = \Delta H = 40$ m

**Step 1 — Apply the equation:**

$$h_f = \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$$

Substitute:

$$40 = \frac{10.67 \cdot 1500 \cdot Q^{1.852}}{110^{1.852} \cdot (0.250)^{4.87}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the constant denominator:**

$$110^{1.852} = 110^{1.852} \approx 5042.4$$

$$(0.250)^{4.87} = 9.537 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$110^{1.852} \cdot (0.250)^{4.87} = 5042.4 \cdot 9.537 \times 10^{-4} = 4.809$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Q^{1.852}$:**

$$Q^{1.852} = \frac{40 \cdot 4.809}{10.67 \cdot 1500} = \frac{192.36}{16005} = 0.01202$$

**Step 4 — Take the $1/1.852$ power:**

$$Q = (0.01202)^{1/1.852} = (0.01202)^{0.5400}$$

$$\log_{10} Q = 0.5400 \times \log_{10}(0.01202) = 0.5400 \times (-1.9202) = -1.0369$$

$$Q = 10^{-1.0369} = 0.0918 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 91.8 \text{ L/s}$$

> **Answer (a): $Q \approx 0.0918$ m³/s = 91.8 L/s. (7 marks — 2 for substitution, 2 for constant evaluation, 2 for power, 1 for final numerical value with unit.)**

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Head loss over 600 m of pipe (linear scaling, since H-W is non-linear in $Q$ but $Q$ is constant here):**

$$h_{f,600} = h_f \cdot \frac{600}{1500} = 40 \cdot 0.4 = 16 \text{ m}$$

(For a single pipe with constant $Q$, head loss is **directly proportional to length**.)

**Step 2 — Total head at J:**

$$H_J = H_{R_1} - h_{f,600} = 120 - 16 = 104 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure head at J:**

$$p_J = H_J - z_J = 104 - 105 = -1 \text{ m}$$

> **A negative pressure head means the HGL is below the pipe elevation — the network would experience sub-atmospheric pressure (vapor cavities), violating CPHEEO norms.**

**Step 4 — Flow velocity:**

$$V = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4 \cdot 0.0918}{\pi \cdot (0.250)^2} = \frac{0.3672}{0.1963} = 1.870 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — CPHEEO check:**

- Velocity 1.870 m/s ∈ [0.6, 2.4] m/s ✓ **OK**
- Pressure at J: $-1$ m ✗ **NOT OK** — negative pressure.

> **Answer (b): Velocity = 1.87 m/s (compliant), but pressure head = $-1$ m (non-compliant). The design fails and must be revised. (7 marks — 2 for head loss by proportion, 2 for pressure calculation, 2 for velocity, 1 for CPHEEO compliance statement.)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 2]`

**Q: A small water-distribution network in a campus has the following data:**

| Pipe | Length (m) | Diameter (mm) | C (H-W) | Flow (L/s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 (R1 → J1) | 800 | 200 | 120 | 15 |
| P2 (J1 → J2) | 400 | 150 | 100 | 8 |
| P3 (J2 → T1) | 600 | 150 | 100 | 8 |
| Service connection at J2 | — | — | — | 4 L/s (withdrawal) |

**Reservoir R1 water level = 50 m, Tank T1 base elevation = 30 m, Junction J1 elevation = 45 m, Junction J2 elevation = 42 m.**

**(a)** Verify the **node-flow continuity** at J1 and J2. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **head loss in each pipe** using Hazen-Williams, and find the **pressure head at J2**. State if it satisfies the **7 m H₂O minimum** at the consumer tap. **(10 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 4 marks

**At Junction J1:**

Inflow from R1 through P1 = 15 L/s
Outflow to J2 through P2 = 8 L/s
Withdrawal at J1 = 15 − 8 = **7 L/s** (this is the demand assigned to J1)

Check:
$$\sum Q_{in} = 15 \text{ L/s}, \quad \sum Q_{out} + D = 8 + 7 = 15 \text{ L/s} \quad \checkmark$$

**At Junction J2:**

Inflow from J1 through P2 = 8 L/s
Outflow to T1 through P3 = 8 L/s
Withdrawal at J2 = 8 − 8 = **0 L/s**? — Wait, the service connection demand is 4 L/s, so:

$$\sum Q_{in} = 8 \text{ L/s}, \quad \sum Q_{out} + D = 8 + 4 = 12 \text{ L/s} \quad \text{✗ NOT balanced}$$

**Resolution:** The flow into J2 must be **12 L/s**, not 8 L/s. Therefore the demand at J1 must be **3 L/s** (since inflow at J1 from R1 is fixed at 15 L/s = 8 L/s to J2 + 7 L/s at J1... but if J2 needs 12 L/s, then P2 = 12 L/s and J1 demand = 15 − 12 = 3 L/s).

> **Re-stated correct network: $Q_{P1} = 15$ L/s, $Q_{P2} = 12$ L/s, $Q_{P3} = 8$ L/s, $D_{J1} = 3$ L/s, $D_{J2} = 4$ L/s. Continuity verified at both nodes. (4 marks)**

#### Part (b) — 10 marks

**Step 1 — Head loss in P1 (L=800 m, D=0.200 m, C=120, Q=0.015 m³/s):**

$$h_{f1} = \frac{10.67 \cdot 800 \cdot (0.015)^{1.852}}{120^{1.852} \cdot (0.200)^{4.87}}$$

Numerator:
$$10.67 \cdot 800 \cdot (0.015)^{1.852} = 8536 \cdot 2.604 \times 10^{-4} = 2.223$$

Denominator:
$$120^{1.852} \cdot (0.200)^{4.87} = 6161.4 \cdot 8.913 \times 10^{-5} = 0.5491$$

$$h_{f1} = \frac{2.223}{0.5491} = 4.048 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Head loss in P2 (L=400 m, D=0.150 m, C=100, Q=0.012 m³/s):**

$$h_{f2} = \frac{10.67 \cdot 400 \cdot (0.012)^{1.852}}{100^{1.852} \cdot (0.150)^{4.87}}$$

Numerator:
$$10.67 \cdot 400 \cdot (0.012)^{1.852} = 4268 \cdot 1.722 \times 10^{-4} = 0.7349$$

Denominator (from previous calculations):
$$100^{1.852} \cdot (0.150)^{4.87} = 6309.6 \cdot 2.516 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5875$$

$$h_{f2} = \frac{0.7349}{1.5875} = 0.463 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Head loss in P3 (L=600 m, D=0.150 m, C=100, Q=0.008 m³/s):**

$$h_{f3} = \frac{10.67 \cdot 600 \cdot (0.008)^{1.852}}{100^{1.852} \cdot (0.150)^{4.87}}$$

Numerator:
$$10.67 \cdot 600 \cdot (0.008)^{1.852} = 6402 \cdot 9.10 \times 10^{-5} = 0.5826$$

Denominator (same as Step 2): 1.5875

$$h_{f3} = \frac{0.5826}{1.5875} = 0.367 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Total head at J2:**

The path R1 → J1 → J2 is hydraulically relevant for J2's pressure (the tank branch is a separate path):

$$H_{J2} = H_{R_1} - h_{f1} - h_{f2} = 50 - 4.048 - 0.463 = 45.489 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Pressure head at J2:**

$$p_{J2} = H_{J2} - z_{J2} = 45.489 - 42.0 = 3.489 \text{ m H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 6 — CPHEEO check:**

Required minimum at consumer tap = 7 m H₂O
Computed = 3.489 m H₂O

> **3.489 m < 7 m → The design DOES NOT satisfy CPHEEO norms.** The student must recommend:
> - Increasing diameter of P1 from 200 mm to 250 mm, **OR**
> - Increasing the reservoir head (raising the ESR), **OR**
> - Adding a booster pump between R1 and J1.

> **Answer (b): $h_{f1} = 4.05$ m, $h_{f2} = 0.46$ m, $h_{f3} = 0.37$ m, $p_{J2} = 3.49$ m. Design FAILS CPHEEO 7 m norm. (10 marks — 1 each for head losses P1, P2, P3; 2 for total head at J2; 2 for pressure; 3 for interpretation and recommendation.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost students 2–4 marks in EPANET lab exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Hazen-Williams.** The constant **10.67 is SI** (m, m³/s, m). Using it with $Q$ in L/s or $D$ in mm gives wrong answers by 2–3 orders of magnitude. Convert everything to metres and m³/s **before** substituting.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $1.852$ exponent on $C$.** Many students write $C^{1.852}$ as $C \cdot 1.852$ or even $C^2$. This is a 2-mark error in any derivation.
>
> 3. **Ignoring node elevation when computing pressure.** Pressure = Total Head − Elevation, NOT just the head loss. Students often quote $H_{J2}$ as if it were the pressure → loses 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Confusing velocity in m/s with L/s.** A common slip is to substitute $Q = 15$ (meaning L/s) into $V = 4Q/(\pi D^2)$ without converting to m³/s.
>
> 5. **Writing continuity without sign convention.** Always state: *inflow positive, outflow negative, demand positive (withdrawal)* — and verify the algebraic sum.
>
> 6. **Not reporting the time step and total duration of EPS.** EPANET defaults are 1-hour steps over 24 hours, but if you changed these, mention it in the lab record.
>
> 7. **Plotting graphs without axis labels and units.** EPANET plots are auto-generated, but a proper lab record must show labelled axes (e.g., *Time (h) on x-axis, Pressure (m H₂O) on y-axis*).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this list and revise 10 minutes before the lab exam.**

### Key Concepts

- ☐ EPANET is a **public-domain** software by **USEPA** for **Extended Period Simulation** of water distribution networks.
- ☐ It solves the **Global Gradient Algorithm (GGA)** by Todini & Pilati (1987), a Newton-Raphson method, NOT the Hardy-Cross method.
- ☐ Three governing equations: **Continuity at nodes**, **Head loss in pipes**, **Tank mass balance**.
- ☐ Three head-loss formulas available: **Hazen-Williams** (default), **Darcy-Weisbach** (rigorous), **Chezy-Manning** (rough pipes).
- ☐ EPANET does **NOT** handle open-channel flow (no free-surface analysis); it is strictly for **pressurized networks**.

### Key Formulas (must memorize)

- ☐ $H = z + p/(\rho g) + V^2/(2g)$ — Total head.
- ☐ $p = H - z$ — Pressure head at any node.
- ☐ $h_f = (10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}) / (C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87})$ — Hazen-Williams (SI units).
- ☐ $V = 4Q / (\pi D^2)$ — Pipe velocity.
- ☐ $A_T \cdot dH_T/dt = \sum Q_p - D_T$ — Tank water-surface ODE.

### Key Design Norms (CPHEEO, India)

- ☐ Minimum residual pressure at consumer: **7 m H₂O** (single-storey) → **12–17 m H₂O** (multi-storey).
- ☐ Velocity range: **0.6 m/s to 2.4 m/s**.
- ☐ Minimum residual chlorine at network extremity: **0.2 mg/L**.

### EPANET Lab Procedure Checklist

- ☐ Step 1: Draw network on the Map View.
- ☐ Step 2: Add Reservoirs, Tanks, Junctions.
- ☐ Step 3: Draw Pipes, Pumps, Valves.
- ☐ Step 4: Assign lengths, diameters, roughness, elevations.
- ☐ Step 5: Assign base demands and demand patterns.
- ☐ Step 6: Select Options → Hydraulics → Hazen-Williams (or as instructed).
- ☐ Step 7: Set Times → Total Duration 24 h, Time Step 1 h.
- ☐ Step 8: Run Analysis (F5).
- ☐ Step 9: Verify all node pressures ≥ 7 m H₂O and velocities in 0.6–2.4 m/s.
- ☐ Step 10: Generate Report (Report → Report Generator) and Plot Graphs.
- ☐ Step 11: Export tabular data for lab record.

### Viva Voce Quick-Fire Questions (with one-line answers)

| # | Question | Answer |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Who developed EPANET? | **U.S. EPA (USEPA)**, Water Supply and Water Resources Division. |
| 2 | What algorithm does EPANET use? | **Global Gradient Algorithm (GGA)** — Todini & Pilati, 1987. |
| 3 | What is the default head-loss formula? | **Hazen-Williams**. |
| 4 | Default unit of pressure in EPANET? | **metres of water (m H₂O)**. |
| 5 | Default unit of flow in EPANET? | **L/s (cubic metres per second internally)**. |
| 6 | Minimum pressure at consumer tap? | **7 m H₂O** (CPHEEO). |
| 7 | What is "EPS"? | **Extended Period Simulation** — time-varying analysis. |
| 8 | File extension of EPANET input file? | **.inp** (text-based). |
| 9 | Can EPANET handle open channels? | **No** — only pressurized networks. |
| 10 | Can EPANET model water quality? | **Yes** — chlorine decay, water age, source tracing. |

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Land use data preparation using GIS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL507 - CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Land use data preparation using GIS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: LAND USE DATA PREPARATION USING GIS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Land Use Data Preparation** is the systematic process of acquiring, pre-processing, classifying, digitizing, and structuring spatial and attribute information about the various uses of land (residential, agricultural, industrial, forest, water bodies, etc.) within a defined geographic area, using Geographic Information System (GIS) tools, in order to generate a thematically accurate, geometrically precise, and topologically clean digital land use/land cover (LULC) database suitable for spatial analysis, planning, and decision-making.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the output of this process is a **vector or raster LULC layer** in which every polygon (or pixel) carries:
- A **spatial component** — its geometry and location on the Earth's surface.
- A **thematic component** — a land use class code following a standard classification system (e.g., Anderson/ USGS Level I or Level II).
- A **topological component** — explicit knowledge of adjacency, containment, and connectivity between spatial features.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy: "The Digital Atlas of a City"**  
> Imagine you are appointed as the chief cartographer of a rapidly growing town. You are handed a stack of old paper maps, a few aerial photographs taken by a drone, satellite images from ISRO's Bhuvan, and field notebooks filled with surveyors' measurements. Your job is to combine all these *messy, mismatched* sources into **one clean, searchable, computer-readable map** that clearly shows where the farms are, where the houses stand, where the lakes lie, and where new roads are proposed.  
>  
> This is exactly what **Land Use Data Preparation using GIS** does. The "messy inputs" are raw raster images, scanned toposheets, and GPS points. The "one clean, searchable map" is a vector LULC layer inside QGIS / ArcGIS where every land parcel is a neat polygon tagged with a land-use label. The GIS software is your digital drafting table, eraser, and colour pencil — all rolled into one.

### 1.3 Key Terminology in Bold

- **LULC** — Land Use / Land Cover.
- **GIS** — Geographic Information System.
- **DEM** — Digital Elevation Model.
- **GCP** — Ground Control Point.
- **RMSE** — Root Mean Square Error (measured in **metres** or **pixels**).
- **NDBI** — Normalized Difference Built-up Index.
- **NDVI** — Normalized Difference Vegetation Index.
- **USGS** — United States Geological Survey (originator of the Anderson Classification).
- **NRSC** — National Remote Sensing Centre, ISRO.
- **GSD** — Ground Sampling Distance.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module Exp 2):**  
> The student is expected to *acquire* a satellite image / toposheet, *georeference* it, *digitize* land use polygons, *populate attribute tables*, and *export* a final LULC map. The end-product must follow a recognised classification scheme.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow — Structured Logical Steps

The complete land use data preparation pipeline consists of **seven sequential stages**. Skipping any stage introduces propagated error into the final output.

**Step 1 — Define Objective & Choose Classification Scheme**
- Decide the *purpose* (urban planning, watershed study, environmental impact assessment).
- Choose a classification level. **Anderson Level I** gives 9 broad classes; **Anderson Level II** gives ~30 detailed sub-classes. For KTU lab work, **Level II** is the expected default.
- Fix the **Minimum Mapping Unit (MMU)** — usually **0.25 ha** for urban and **1.0 ha** for rural LULC studies (NRSC guideline).

**Step 2 — Data Acquisition**
- **Primary sources**: Field survey using GPS, drone UAV orthomosaics.
- **Secondary sources**: 
  - Satellite imagery: **Landsat-8/9** (30 m), **Sentinel-2** (10 m), **Cartosat** (2.5 m), **LISS-IV** (5.8 m).
  - Topographic sheets: Survey of India (SOI) at **1:50,000** or **1:25,000** scale.
  - Cadastral maps: Village / taluk records.
  - Open portals: **Bhuvan, USGS EarthExplorer, Copernicus Open Access Hub**.

**Step 3 — Pre-Processing**
- **Geometric correction / Georeferencing** — assign real-world coordinates to a scanned map or raw image.
- **Radiometric correction** — convert Digital Numbers (DN) to Top-of-Atmosphere (TOA) or Surface Reflectance.
- **Image enhancement** — contrast stretching, histogram equalisation, band compositing (FCC: NIR-Red-Green).
- **Mosaicking & Sub-setting** — stitching multiple tiles; clipping to Area of Interest (AOI).
- **Cloud masking** — remove pixels contaminated by cloud / shadow.

**Step 4 — Image Classification (for raster-based LULC)**
- **Supervised classification**: Maximum Likelihood, SVM, Random Forest, Minimum Distance.
- **Unsupervised classification**: K-Means, ISODATA.
- **Object-based** (OBIA): eCognition-style segmentation followed by classification.
- **Hybrid**: Knowledge-based + indices (NDVI for vegetation, NDBI for built-up, NDWI for water).

**Step 5 — Vectorisation / On-screen Digitisation**
- For vector output (preferred for civil-engineering plans), **digitise** polygon boundaries on-screen by tracing the classified raster or visually interpreted image.
- Create a shapefile/GeoPackage with feature classes: Built-up, Agriculture, Forest, Water, Wasteland, etc.

**Step 6 — Attribute Data Creation & Topology**
- Open the attribute table and add fields: `Class_Name`, `Area_ha`, `Perimeter_m`, `Source_Year`, `Accuracy`.
- Build **topology rules**: no overlaps, no gaps, no slivers, closed polygons.
- Compute geometry: area in **hectares (ha)** and perimeter in **metres (m)**.

**Step 7 — Accuracy Assessment & Map Production**
- Generate **stratified random samples** of ground truth points (minimum **50 per class** per Anderson guidelines).
- Construct a **Confusion Matrix**; compute **Overall Accuracy** and **Kappa Coefficient (κ)**.
- Layout the map with: title, legend, north arrow, scale bar, coordinate grid, projection info, data source, date.

> [!NOTE]  
> **"Why these steps?"** Each step removes a specific kind of error. Georeferencing removes *positional error*; classification removes *thematic error*; topology removes *logical error*; accuracy assessment removes *confidence ambiguity*. Without all four, the dataset is unfit for KTU-graded civil engineering applications.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Application in LULC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $RMSE = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[(x_{i}-x_{i}')^{2}+(y_{i}-y_{i}')^{2}\right]}{n}}$ | $x_i,y_i$ = GCP measured; $x_i',y_i'$ = GCP truth | **metres / pixels** | Georeferencing accuracy check |
| 2 | $OA = \dfrac{\sum \text{Diagonal elements of confusion matrix}}{\text{Total samples}} \times 100$ | OA = Overall Accuracy | **%** | Classification accuracy |
| 3 | $\kappa = \dfrac{N \sum x_{ii} - \sum (x_{i+} \cdot x_{+i})}{N^{2} - \sum (x_{i+} \cdot x_{+i})}$ | $N$ = total; $x_{ii}$ = diagonal; $x_{i+}$ = row total; $x_{+i}$ = col total | dimensionless (0–1) | Kappa agreement statistic |
| 4 | $A = \dfrac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_{i} y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_{i}) \right\vert$ | Shoelace formula for polygon | **m² or ha** | Polygon area computation |
| 5 | $P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{(x_{i+1}-x_{i})^{2}+(y_{i+1}-y_{i})^{2}}$ | Perimeter of polygon | **m** | Perimeter computation |
| 6 | $GSD = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Altitude} \times \text{Pixel Pitch}}{\text{Focal Length}}$ | GSD = Ground Sampling Distance | **m / pixel** | Image resolution planning |
| 7 | $NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$ | Vegetation index | dimensionless (−1 to +1) | Vegetation extraction |
| 8 | $NDBI = \dfrac{SWIR - NIR}{SWIR + NIR}$ | Built-up index | dimensionless | Built-up extraction |
| 9 | $NDWI = \dfrac{Green - NIR}{Green + NIR}$ | Water index | dimensionless | Water-body extraction |
| 10 | $\text{Map Scale} = \dfrac{\text{Ground Distance}}{\text{Map Distance}}$ | Linear scale | unitless | Determining display scale |
| 11 | $\text{Producer's Accuracy (class } j) = \dfrac{x_{jj}}{x_{+j}} \times 100$ | Row-based | **%** | Class-wise accuracy |
| 12 | $\text{User's Accuracy (class } j) = \dfrac{x_{jj}}{x_{j+}} \times 100$ | Column-based | **%** | Class-wise reliability |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Utility:**  
> LULC data is the **base layer** for almost every modern civil engineering study: hydrological modelling (curve number depends on land use), transportation planning (route alignment), urban heat island studies, flood-risk mapping, and environmental impact assessment. A KTU student mastering this lab can directly apply the output in subsequent semesters' projects (PCCVL508 Modelling Lab II, project work, and MTech research).

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Urban Local Bodies** (Kerala: Corporation of Kochi, Trivandrum) use LULC layers to compute **property tax zones**, parking demand, and storm-water network design.
- **KSREC & CWC** use LULC + DEM for **flood inundation modelling** of the Periyar and Pamba basins.
- **National Informatics Centre (NIC)** uses LULC for **asset mapping** under the SVAMITVA scheme.
- **Smart City Mission** (Kochi, Coimbatore) depends on LULC to track urban sprawl year-on-year.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) for Georeferencing

**Problem Context:** While georeferencing a scanned SOI toposheet, the user places **n** Ground Control Points (GCPs). For the *i-th* GCP, the original map coordinate is $(x_i, y_i)$ and the coordinate read from the reference system (Google Earth / DGPS) is $(x_i', y_i')$. Derive the RMSE used to assess positional accuracy.

**Step 1 — Compute the squared linear residual for the *i-th* GCP:**

$$
d_i^{2} = (x_{i}-x_{i}')^{2} + (y_{i}-y_{i}')^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the squared residuals for all *n* GCPs:**

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_{i}^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[(x_{i}-x_{i}')^{2}+(y_{i}-y_{i}')^{2}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Take the mean (divide by *n*):**

$$
\overline{d^{2}} = \dfrac{S}{n} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[(x_{i}-x_{i}')^{2}+(y_{i}-y_{i}')^{2}\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root to obtain the Root Mean Square Error:**

$$
RMSE = \sqrt{\overline{d^{2}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[(x_{i}-x_{i}')^{2}+(y_{i}-y_{i}')^{2}\right]}{n}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU-style):**

Suppose 4 GCPs give residuals in metres:

| GCP # | $x_i - x_i'$ (m) | $y_i - y_i'$ (m) | $d_i^2$ (m²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 3 | 13 |
| 2 | 1 | 2 | 5 |
| 3 | 3 | 1 | 10 |
| 4 | 2 | 2 | 8 |
| **Σ** | | | **36** |

Substitute into the derived formula:

$$
RMSE = \sqrt{\dfrac{36}{4}} = \sqrt{9} = 3.0\ \text{m}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Interpretation:** The total positional error of the georeferenced map is **3.0 m**. For a 1:25,000 SOI toposheet (whose plottable accuracy ≈ 12.5 m), this is excellent. KTU valuation accepts RMSE < **one pixel** of the source image.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Kappa Coefficient (κ) from Confusion Matrix

**Problem Context:** After supervised classification of a Sentinel-2 image, 150 ground-truth points are collected and compared against classified labels. A 3 × 3 confusion matrix is built (Classes A, B, C). Derive κ.

**Step 1 — Define the confusion matrix elements (an *n × n* matrix where *n* is the number of classes):**

- $x_{ii}$ = number of correctly classified samples of class *i* (diagonal element).
- $x_{i+}$ = row total (sum of row *i*) = total samples of class *i* on the ground.
- $x_{+i}$ = column total (sum of column *i*) = total samples classified as class *i*.
- $N$ = grand total of samples = $\sum_{i} \sum_{j} x_{ij}$.

**Step 2 — Observed agreement (P_o):**

$$
P_{o} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ii}}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Expected agreement by chance (P_e):**

$$
P_{e} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_{i+} \cdot x_{+i})}{N^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Kappa as 1 minus the ratio of chance-expected disagreement to observed disagreement:**

$$
\kappa = \dfrac{P_{o} - P_{e}}{1 - P_{e}} = \dfrac{N \sum x_{ii} - \sum (x_{i+} \cdot x_{+i})}{N^{2} - \sum (x_{i+} \cdot x_{+i})}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked example:**

Given confusion matrix (samples):

|  | Class A (pred) | Class B (pred) | Class C (pred) | Row Total $x_{i+}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Class A (true) | 42 | 4 | 4 | 50 |
| Class B (true) | 5 | 38 | 7 | 50 |
| Class C (true) | 3 | 8 | 39 | 50 |
| **Col Total $x_{+i}$** | 50 | 50 | 50 | **N = 150** |

Compute $\sum x_{ii}$ (diagonal sum):

$$
\sum x_{ii} = 42 + 38 + 39 = 119
$$

Compute $\sum (x_{i+} \cdot x_{+i})$:

$$
\sum (x_{i+} \cdot x_{+i}) = (50 \times 50) + (50 \times 50) + (50 \times 50) = 7500
$$

Substitute into Kappa formula:

$$
\kappa = \dfrac{150 \times 119 - 7500}{150^{2} - 7500} = \dfrac{17850 - 7500}{22500 - 7500} = \dfrac{10350}{15000} = 0.69
$$

**Interpretation:** κ = 0.69 indicates **substantial agreement** (Landis & Koch scale: 0.61–0.80). For KTU lab acceptance, κ ≥ **0.80** is desirable.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Polygon Area Using the Shoelace Formula

**Step 1 — For an ordered list of *n* vertices $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \dots, (x_n, y_n)$ with the closing vertex $(x_{n+1}, y_{n+1}) = (x_1, y_1)$, the signed area is given by:**

$$
2A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_{i}\, y_{i+1} - x_{i+1}\, y_{i})
$$

**Step 2 — Take the absolute value and halve to get area:**

$$
A = \dfrac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_{i}\, y_{i+1} - x_{i+1}\, y_{i}) \right\vert
$$

**Step 3 — Unit conversion from m² to hectares (1 ha = 10 000 m²):**

$$
A_{ha} = \dfrac{A_{m^{2}}}{10000}
$$

**Step 4 — Quick numerical example (square plot of side 100 m in UTM):**

Vertices: (0, 0), (100, 0), (100, 100), (0, 100).

$$
2A = (0 \cdot 0 - 100 \cdot 0) + (100 \cdot 100 - 100 \cdot 0) + (100 \cdot 100 - 0 \cdot 100) + (0 \cdot 0 - 0 \cdot 100)
$$

$$
2A = 0 + 10000 + 10000 + 0 = 20000 \quad\Rightarrow\quad A = 10000\ \text{m}^{2} = 1.0\ \text{ha}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why this matters for LULC:** Anderson Level II classification demands **per-polygon area reporting**. The Shoelace formula gives exact area for any simple polygon, and GIS software (QGIS field calculator) implements it directly.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Source vs. Data Type vs. Typical Use

| Data Source | Type | Spatial Resolution | Scale | Typical LULC Use | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Landsat-8/9 OLI** | Raster | 30 m | 1:250 000 | Regional LULC, temporal change | Free, 16-day repeat | Coarse for urban |
| **Sentinel-2 MSI** | Raster | 10 m | 1:100 000 | District-level LULC | Free, 5-day repeat, 13 bands | Cloud cover issue |
| **Cartosat-1** | Pan | 2.5 m | 1:25 000 | Town planning | High resolution | Stereo-pair needed for DEM |
| **LISS-IV (Resourcesat)** | Multispectral | 5.8 m | 1:50 000 | Urban LULC | Indian sensor, free for govt | Limited swath |
| **Drone UAV** | Raster (Ortho-mosaic) | 5–10 cm | 1:500 | Campus, ward-level studies | Very high resolution | Small coverage |
| **SOI Toposheet** | Vector (scanned) | Plottable 0.25 mm | 1:25 000 / 50 000 | Historical LULC baseline | Georeferenced, accurate | Outdated (often > 20 yrs) |
| **GPS / DGPS Survey** | Vector (point) | ±2–5 m | variable | Ground truth, GCP | Real-time, accurate | Slow, labour intensive |
| **Google Earth Pro** | Raster | Variable | variable | Quick visual digitisation | Free, easy | Not for publication |

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU tip:** Most KTU labs use **Sentinel-2 (free) + SOI toposheet (1:50 000) + GPS field points** because they cost nothing and are easy to obtain. The expected output is a digitised LULC map at 1:50 000 scale with at least 5 classes.
