## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Define **Land Use / Land Cover (LULC)**. List any **four** Level I classes of the Anderson Classification System.

**Model Answer:**  
LULC refers to the categorisation of the Earth's surface based on its **biophysical cover** (land cover) and the **human purpose** for which it is used (land use). The two terms together describe what is physically present and how it is functionally employed.  
The four Level I classes of the Anderson Classification are:
1. Urban or Built-up Land.
2. Agricultural Land.
3. Forest Land.
4. Water.

(Any four of the nine: Urban, Agricultural, Rangeland, Forest, Water, Wetland, Barren, Tundra, Perennial Snow/Ice — full credit.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
What is **Georeferencing**? Why is it an essential pre-processing step in land use data preparation?

**Model Answer:**  
Georeferencing is the process of **assigning real-world geographic coordinates** (latitude / longitude or projected X / Y) to a raster image, scanned map, or aerial photograph by aligning it with a known coordinate system using Ground Control Points (GCPs).  
It is essential because:
- A raw satellite image or scanned toposheet has only **pixel-row, pixel-column** coordinates, not real-world positions.
- Without georeferencing, the image cannot be **overlaid** with other spatial layers (cadastral, road, drainage) for combined analysis.
- It enables **measurement of true distances, areas, and directions**.
- It provides the geometric foundation for **digitisation and classification** to produce a spatially accurate LULC layer.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 scheme, Part B of a Module Experiment question carries **14 marks** and offers internal choice. The student must answer either **Question A** or **Question B**. Each question is split into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **step-by-step procedure** to prepare a Land Use / Land Cover (LULC) map of a chosen watershed using open-source GIS software (QGIS). Include data acquisition, pre-processing, classification, and digitisation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A student performs supervised classification of a Sentinel-2 image into three classes (Built-up, Vegetation, Water) and collects **120 ground-truth samples**. The resulting confusion matrix is given below. Calculate the **Overall Accuracy** and the **Kappa Coefficient (κ)**. Comment on the classification quality.

|  | Built-up (pred) | Vegetation (pred) | Water (pred) | Row Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Built-up (true) | 35 | 3 | 2 | 40 |
| Vegetation (true) | 4 | 32 | 4 | 40 |
| Water (true) | 1 | 5 | 34 | 40 |
| **Column Total** | 40 | 40 | 40 | **120** |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-step procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Define objective and AOI (1 mark):** Fix the watershed boundary; decide on classification level (Anderson Level II is preferred for watershed studies). Set MMU = 0.5 ha.
2. **Data acquisition (1 mark):** Download a cloud-free **Sentinel-2** image (10 m) from Copernicus Open Access Hub; obtain SOI toposheet (1:50 000) of the area; collect GPS field points.
3. **Pre-processing (2 marks):**
   - Open the image in QGIS; **subset to AOI** using clip raster.
   - Apply **atmospheric correction** (e.g., DOS1 in QGIS SCP plugin) to obtain surface reflectance.
   - **Georeference** the toposheet using GCPs from Google Earth (target RMSE < 10 m).
   - Generate a **False Colour Composite (FCC)** with bands NIR-Red-Green for visual interpretation.
4. **Classification (2 marks):**
   - Take **training samples** for each class (minimum 30 pixels per class).
   - Apply the **Random Forest** or **Maximum Likelihood** classifier (QGIS SCP plugin / Orfeo ToolBox).
   - Convert raster to vector using `Polygonize`.
5. **Digitisation and attribute entry (1 mark):**
   - On-screen digitise if classification is imperfect; clean slivers.
   - Add fields: `Class_Name`, `Area_ha`, `Perimeter_m`; compute geometry.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Overall Accuracy:**

$$
OA = \dfrac{\text{Diagonal sum}}{\text{Total samples}} \times 100 = \dfrac{35 + 32 + 34}{120} \times 100
$$

$$
OA = \dfrac{101}{120} \times 100 = 84.17\ \%
$$

**Step 2 — Expected agreement by chance:**

$$
\sum (x_{i+} \cdot x_{+i}) = (40 \times 40) + (40 \times 40) + (40 \times 40) = 4800
$$

**Step 3 — Kappa:**

$$
\kappa = \dfrac{N \sum x_{ii} - \sum (x_{i+} \cdot x_{+i})}{N^{2} - \sum (x_{i+} \cdot x_{+i})} = \dfrac{120 \times 101 - 4800}{120^{2} - 4800}
$$

$$
\kappa = \dfrac{12120 - 4800}{14400 - 4800} = \dfrac{7320}{9600} = 0.7625
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

- **Overall Accuracy = 84.17 %** — Good (target ≥ 85 %).
- **Kappa = 0.7625** — *Substantial agreement* (Landis & Koch: 0.61–0.80). For a *good* classification, κ ≥ 0.80 is the KTU benchmark. The classifier can be improved by adding more training samples and applying post-classification filtering (majority / clump sieve).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module Exp 2]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Anderson Classification System** of land use / land cover. With a neat diagram, describe the **Level I and Level II** hierarchy and give **two examples** for each of the categories: Urban, Agricultural, Forest, and Water.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A scanned SOI toposheet is georeferenced using **5 Ground Control Points (GCPs)**. The residuals (in metres) between map coordinates and reference coordinates are tabulated. Compute the **RMSE** of georeferencing and state whether the toposheet is acceptable for a **1:50 000** LULC mapping project (acceptable RMSE ≤ 12.5 m for 1:50 000 scale).

| GCP # | $x_i - x_i'$ (m) | $y_i - y_i'$ (m) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 3 |
| 2 | 2 | 1 |
| 3 | 3 | 4 |
| 4 | 1 | 2 |
| 5 | 2 | 3 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Anderson Classification (7 marks):**

The **Anderson Classification System**, developed by James R. Anderson and colleagues in **1976** for the **USGS**, is a hierarchical land use / land cover classification. It has:
- **Level I** — 9 broad categories (used for national / regional mapping at scales 1:250 000 to 1:500 000).
- **Level II** — ~30 sub-categories (used for state / district mapping at scales 1:50 000 to 1:100 000).
- **Level III & IV** — further sub-divisions for very detailed local studies (1:5 000 to 1:20 000).

**Level I (9 classes) with two examples each:**

| Level I | Level II Example 1 | Level II Example 2 |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Urban or Built-up | Residential | Commercial and Services |
| 2. Agricultural | Cropland and Pasture | Orchards, Groves, Vineyards |
| 3. Forest Land | Deciduous Forest | Evergreen Forest |
| 4. Water | Streams and Canals | Lakes, Reservoirs |

**Diagram (text-rendered since it is already in the Mermaid block of Section 4.3):**

```
Level I (9 classes)
   ├── 1 Urban or Built-up
   │      ├── Residential
   │      ├── Commercial / Services
   │      ├── Industrial
   │      ├── Transportation
   │      └── Mixed Urban
   ├── 2 Agricultural
   │      ├── Cropland / Pasture
   │      ├── Orchards / Groves
   │      └── Confined Feeding
   ├── 3 Forest Land
   │      ├── Deciduous
   │      ├── Evergreen
   │      └── Mixed
   ├── 4 Water
   │      ├── Streams / Canals
   │      └── Lakes
   └── … (5 more Level I classes)
```

**(b) RMSE computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $d_i^2$ for each GCP:**

$$
d_i^2 = (x_i - x_i')^2 + (y_i - y_i')^2
$$

| GCP # | $\Delta x$ | $\Delta y$ | $d_i^2$ (m²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 3 | $16 + 9 = 25$ |
| 2 | 2 | 1 | $4 + 1 = 5$ |
| 3 | 3 | 4 | $9 + 16 = 25$ |
| 4 | 1 | 2 | $1 + 4 = 5$ |
| 5 | 2 | 3 | $4 + 9 = 13$ |
| **Σ** | | | **73** |

**Step 2 — Compute RMSE:**

$$
RMSE = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum d_i^2}{n}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{73}{5}} = \sqrt{14.6}
$$

$$
RMSE \approx 3.82\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision (1 mark):**

- Computed RMSE = **3.82 m**.
- Acceptable limit for 1:50 000 scale = **12.5 m**.
- Since 3.82 m < 12.5 m, the toposheet is **acceptable** for the LULC mapping project. ✔

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**  
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the classification level** in the answer (Level I vs Level II) — loses **1 mark** every time. Always write *which level* you are using and *why*.  
> 2. **Mixing up Producer's and User's accuracy** in the confusion matrix — remember: Producer's accuracy uses the **row total** (denominator), User's accuracy uses the **column total**.  
> 3. **Skipping the unit conversion** (m² → ha) in area calculations — KTU strictly expects **Area in hectares** for LULC reports.  
> 4. **Using only 2–3 GCPs** in georeferencing — minimum **5 well-distributed GCPs** is the KTU bench-mark; **fewer = 1 mark deduction**.  
> 5. **Ignoring the projection / datum** in the final map layout — every map MUST carry the projection (e.g., WGS 84 / UTM Zone 44N) and the data source / year. Missing this loses **1 mark** in the map production stage.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **LULC** = Land Use / Land Cover; preparation = converting raw imagery + ground data into a clean digital layer.
- **Anderson Classification** is the KTU-default; **Level II** has ~30 classes and is expected for most lab work.
- **Minimum Mapping Unit (MMU)**: **0.25 ha** (urban), **1.0 ha** (rural) as per NRSC.
- **Seven-stage pipeline**: Define → Acquire → Pre-process → Classify → Digitise → Attribute + Topology → Accuracy + Map.
- **Free data sources** for KTU labs: **Sentinel-2 (10 m)**, **Landsat-8/9 (30 m)**, **SOI toposheets (1:50 000)**, **Bhuvan, Google Earth, OpenStreetMap**.
- **Georeferencing accuracy** is measured by **RMSE**; acceptable RMSE < **one pixel** of the source image.
- **Spectral indices**: 
  - NDVI → Vegetation.
  - NDBI → Built-up.
  - NDWI → Water.
- **Classification accuracy** is measured by **Overall Accuracy** (≥ 85 % acceptable) and **Kappa** (κ ≥ 0.80 acceptable).
- **Confusion Matrix** → Diagonal sum / N = Overall Accuracy; use the full Kappa formula for chance-corrected agreement.
- **Shoelace formula** for polygon area; remember to convert m² → **hectares (÷ 10 000)**.
- **Topology rules**: no overlaps, no gaps, no slivers, polygons must close.
- **Map layout MUST contain**: Title, Legend, North arrow, Scale bar, Coordinate grid, Projection (WGS 84 / UTM), Data source, Date.
- **Common software**: **QGIS (free), ArcGIS (paid), GRASS GIS, SAGA GIS, ERDAS Imagine, ENVI**.
- **Kerala-specific note**: Coastal LULC studies in Kerala must include the **Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ)** classes; backwaters and paddy fields are major LULC types.
- **Submission requirement (KTU 2024)**: Final LULC map + soft copy (GeoPackage / Shapefile) + accuracy report + 1-page methodology note.

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE EXPERIMENT 2**
